examples.value: The Cu-HDD catalyst delivers a C2+ Faradaic efficiency of over 90% at a current density of 727 mA cm−2 in a flow cell system, reaching total current density up to 1 A cm−2 with C2+ Faradaic efficiency over 80%. The C2+ current normalized to electrochemical surface area of Cu-HDD is about seven times larger than Cu-LDD and more than 13 times larger than Cu-NC at −0.795 V versus RHE.

examples.value: In the flow cell system using highly alkaline electrolyte (1 m KOH), poor long-term performance is reported due to loss of hydrophobicity of the gas diffusion electrodes (GDEs) under alkaline conditions, leading to flooding and preventing diffusion of CO and gas products through the GDE.

For a face-centered cubic (fcc) noble-metal crystal structure, the surface facets can be classified as low-index facets, including the three basic (111), (100), and (110) facets, and high-index facets (hkl) (h ≥ k ≥ l > 0). Nanocrystals with low-index planes feature high coordination numbers and relatively low catalytic performance, while nanostructured exposed facets with high surface energies are beneficial for boosting catalytic performance. High-index facets have many terraces, steps, kinks, edges, vertexes, and corners that possess abundant defect sites and low-coordinate active sites, exhibiting attractive catalytic properties. Electrocatalytic reactions occur on the surface of the catalyst, which is key to heterogeneous electrocatalytic reactions. There are mainly three interfaces: the inner boundary between components in a hybrid catalyst, the interface between the electrolyte and the catalyst, and the interface between the catalyst and the electrode or other substrate materials. Rationally designing and controlling the interface structures of electrocatalysts is key to enhancing catalytic performance by increasing active sites, facilitating synergistic reactions, and improving contact between electrolyte and catalyst.

Metal-metal interfaces produce synergistic effects at nanoscale interfaces, enhancing catalytic performance. Metal-metal hybrids with different structures, morphologies, dimensions, and compositions have been fabricated and proven to improve electrocatalytic activity due to synergistic effects. For example, Pt and Pt3Ni nanoparticles grown on Au nanowires showed better activity and durability in ORR than Pt/C catalysts. Precise interface engineering of metal-metal heterostructures can achieve more efficient electrocatalysts by exposing more active sites.

Core-shell-type noble-metal nanocrystals have distinct geometric structures; lattice mismatch between core and shell generates lattice strain on the surface. Changing internal and external geometric configurations can improve catalytic performance via ligand and/or strain effects. Numerous core-shell nanostructures have been developed, including nanoparticles, one-dimensional nanocable structures, and two-dimensional architectures. For example, uniform PtPb/Pt core-shell nanoplates with large biaxial tensile strain boosted ORR performance.

Metal-oxide interfaces prevent agglomeration of metal catalysts and expose more active sites. Metal nanoparticles coupled with oxides generate metal-support interfaces and strong coupling interactions, promoting catalyst stability and enhancing electron conductivity. For example, PdCu nanoparticles coupled with oxygen-deficient perovskite-type WO2.72 nanorods exhibited robust electrocatalytic performance in formic acid oxidation. Metal hydroxides also enhance electrocatalysis; Ni(OH) nanoclusters on Pt electrodes enhance HER activity by facilitating water dissociation in basic media.

Metal-dichalcogenide interfaces can give rise to more active sites and enhance electrocatalytic performance. Noble-metal nanoparticles on single-layer MoS2 nanosheets have been developed through epitaxial growth, exhibiting enhanced HER performance. Pt3Ni/NiS heterostructures with high density of exposed interfaces activate the HO–H bond and promote water dissociation, while Pt3Ni core converts H+ to H2. The components cooperate synergistically to enhance HER catalysis. Interface engineering in metal-dichalcogenide structures is an efficient strategy for boosting electrocatalytic performance.

The ORR activity on typical low-index facets of Pt follows the trend Pt(110) > Pt(111) > Pt(100) in weakly adsorbing electrolytes, but changes in H2SO4 due to strong adsorption of SO4^2- on Pt(111). High-index-faceted Pt shows enhanced ORR activity, with active sites located at stepped edges of (111) terraces, such as (221) and (331).

PtNi(111) single-crystal surface shows much higher ORR activity than PtNi(110) and PtNi(100), with specific activity surpassing commercial Pt/C by nearly two orders of magnitude, attributed to lower hydroxyl species coverage.

Pt3Ni octahedral nanoparticles enclosed by (111) facets have been synthesized with sizes down to several nanometers, achieving high ORR activity; doping Pt3Ni octahedra with Mo further enhances ORR activity and stability.

Core-shell PtM nanocrystals with pure Pt shell and M or PtM core enhance ORR performance by maximizing Pt utilization and intrinsic activity via ligand and strain effects; compressive strain on Pt shell promotes ORR rate.
Hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) at the anode is severely deactivated by ppm-level CO impurities in reformate hydrogen.
Ru cores promote CO tolerance of Pt surface by preferential CO oxidation in hydrogen feeds on Ru@Pt nanoparticles compared with Ru-Pt alloys or Pt shells with other metal cores.
Hydrogen feed containing ppm-level CO impurities deactivates Pt catalysts; reformate hydrogen with about 10 ppm CO and air bleeding is targeted to reduce CO poisoning in PEM fuel cells.
Ru@Pt core-shell catalysts with bilayer Pt shells show enhanced CO tolerance and higher current density at 0.65 V compared to Pt/C baseline in PEM fuel cells with CO-containing hydrogen feed.
Bilayer Ru@Pt nanoparticles achieve superior durability and excellent CO tolerance for HOR at ultra-low catalyst loadings (25 μg cm⁻² Pt, 10 μg cm⁻² Ru).
Ru is prone to dissolution under oxidative conditions, which can degrade catalyst performance and cause Ru ion migration through electrolyte membranes.

examples.section_title: 7 Pacific Northwest National Laboratory,902 Battelle Blvd, Richland,Washington 99352,USA
examples.value: Cu-YSZ cermets prepared by calcining mixtures of CuO and YSZ are unstable in reducing atmospheres above 650 °C due to Cu mobility. The Cu/CeO system achieves improved thermal stability by first obtaining a porous skeleton by ceramic methods, then adding Cu and ceria in subsequent low-temperature steps, resulting in stable operation up to 800 °C.

examples.section_title: 7 Pacific Northwest National Laboratory,902 Battelle Blvd, Richland,Washington 99352,USA
examples.value: The Cu forms an intimate near-continuous thin network within the retaining skeleton, with some carbon deposition enhancing conduction by bridging links between the Cu network. The anode electrolyte microstructure is the dominant feature governing fuel electrode activity, emphasizing the importance of controlled interfacial and microstructural engineering.

examples.section_title: 7 Pacific Northwest National Laboratory,902 Battelle Blvd, Richland,Washington 99352,USA
examples.value: In the commonly used Ni/YSZ cermet anode, nickel acts as catalyst for hydrogen oxidation and electrical current conductor. Nickel is also highly active for steam-reforming of methane, enabling internal reforming SOFCs operating on methane and water mixtures. However, nickel catalyzes carbon filament formation from hydrocarbons under reducing conditions, which can destroy the anode unless sufficient steam is present to remove carbon faster than it forms.

examples.section_title: 7 Pacific Northwest National Laboratory,902 Battelle Blvd, Richland,Washington 99352,USA
examples.value: One approach to overcoming nickel anode limitations is adding oxide-based oxidation catalysts such as yttria-doped ceria interlayers, increasing activity for electrochemical oxidation of methane. However, operating temperature must be maintained below 700 °C to suppress carbon deposition on nickel.

examples.section_title: 7 Pacific Northwest National Laboratory,902 Battelle Blvd, Richland,Washington 99352,USA
examples.value: Three strategies for direct conversion of hydrocarbon fuels are: (1) using conventional nickel-cermet anodes with modified operating conditions to slow carbon deposition; (2) replacing nickel cermets with copper-ceria composites that are inert to carbon formation and stable with hydrocarbons; (3) using electronically conductive ceramic anodes tolerant to carbon formation, such as La-doped strontium titanate and doped lanthanum chromites.

examples.section_title: 7 Pacific Northwest National Laboratory,902 Battelle Blvd, Richland,Washington 99352,USA
examples.value: More basic work to probe the mechanistic details of fuel utilisation reactions needs to be undertaken, as these details are extremely important to rational design of more efficient anodes and thus high-performance SOFCs powered by practical fuels.

examples.section_title: 7 Pacific Northwest National Laboratory,902 Battelle Blvd, Richland,Washington 99352,USA
examples.value: Nickel-cermet anodes provide high current densities and good performance on hydrogen but suffer from carbon deposition when operating directly on hydrocarbons, requiring steam reforming. Copper-ceria based anodes show good resistance to carbon deposition and can operate directly on hydrocarbons but have lower catalytic activity, resulting in higher current densities on hydrogen than hydrocarbons.

examples.section_title: 7 Pacific Northwest National Laboratory,902 Battelle Blvd, Richland,Washington 99352,USA
examples.value: Ceramic anodes such as La-doped strontium titanate and doped lanthanum chromites show reasonable performance for direct use of methane and other hydrocarbons, with additions like ceria or small amounts of nickel enhancing performance. However, catalytic properties and mechanisms are not fully understood and require further study.

examples.section_title: 7 Pacific Northwest National Laboratory,902 Battelle Blvd, Richland,Washington 99352,USA
examples.value: Polarization resistance of chromite perovskites is generally too high for efficient SOFC operation, though doping improves catalytic properties. Nickel-substituted lanthanum chromite shows lowest polarization resistances but may suffer from nickel exsolution raising stability concerns.

examples.section_title: 7 Pacific Northwest National Laboratory,902 Battelle Blvd, Richland,Washington 99352,USA
examples.value: New fuel electrode formulations for second-generation commercial fuel cells can offer improved redox tolerance and better resistance stability in hydrocarbon fuels. Oxidation catalysts such as ceria or lanthanum chromium manganite and reforming catalysts such as nickel- or ruthenium-doped lanthanum chromite have been identified. Electronic conductivity in the anode current collector has been addressed in oxides based on strontium titanate.

There are four types of electrochemical coproduction: parallel, convergent, divergent, and linear paired electrolysis. Parallel paired electrolysis features simultaneous occurrence of two unrelated half-reactions in a divided cell. Convergent paired electrolysis produces a single product from intermediates formed at cathode and anode in an undivided cell. Divergent paired electrolysis uses a common starting substrate at both electrodes to produce different products. Linear paired electrolysis produces the same product from the same substrate via different electrochemical reactions.

The study selected 16 cathodic reactions including CO2RR and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), and 18 anodic reactions including OOR and oxygen evolution reaction (OER), categorized based on feedstock source and reaction characteristics. A commercial electrolyzer with parallel paired electrolysis was considered for process modeling.

Global sensitivity analysis (GSA) was performed to quantify effects of current density, Faraday efficiency (FE), and overpotential on levelized cost of chemicals (LCC). The analysis revealed that LCC sensitivity varies by product and process combination, with FE generally a key factor due to its impact on operating expenditures.
At lab scale, ethylene production from CO2 can achieve 70% Faraday efficiency at a high current density of 100 mA cm−2, indicating technological possibility for practical implementation.

The levelized cost of chemicals (LCC) is sensitive to current density and Faraday efficiency (FE). At very low current densities (<100 mA cm−2), LCC is highly sensitive to current density due to capital expenditure changes. FE sensitivity varies by product; low-economy CO2RR products are highly sensitive to FE. Overpotential also affects LCC but was treated independently in the analysis.

Technological advancements at very low performance levels (low current density and FE) yield rapid improvements in economic feasibility, but these effects diminish after a certain technology maturity is reached.

Performance metrics include solar-to-chemical efficiency (STC), production rates at cathode and anode units, CO2 conversion, and required photovoltaic and electrolyzer cell areas. Equations relate current density, Faraday efficiency, standard reduction potentials, and overpotentials to production rates and electrolyzer area. Solar energy availability is calculated from photovoltaic farm capacity and solar capacity factor.

The cathode and anode were separated by a porous solid electrolyte (PSE) layer, where electrochemically generated formate and proton were recombined to form molecular formic acid. Gas diffusion layer (GDL) electrodes coated with CO2RR and HOR catalysts were used as cathode and anode to improve mass transfer of CO2 and H2. Both catalysts were in close contact with anion and cation exchange membranes (AEM and CEM) respectively for efficient ion transportation.

Electrochemical CO2 reduction reaction (CO2RR) to formate occurs on the cathode where CO2 is selectively reduced to formate ions on a selective CO2RR catalyst. On the anode side, hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) is performed by feeding H2 gas stream to release protons. Electrochemically generated HCOO− and H+ are driven by electric field to move across AEM or CEM into the PSE layer and recombine to form HCOOH molecule.

Using an inert N2 gas flow instead of DI water flow to carry away formic acid vapors avoids accumulation of products within the solid electrolyte layer, maintaining high Faradaic efficiency and selectivity. The gas flow can be kept high to prevent product buildup, and high concentration formic acid can be obtained by simple cold-condensation downstream.

The AEM could be partially neutralized by CO2 gas during CO2RR, leading to decreased ionic conductivity and increased cell voltage over time. The degradation of AEM is a cause of performance decay at high current densities.

The nBuLi-Bi catalyst achieved formate partial current densities greater than 440 mA cm−2 with maximal Faradaic efficiency of ~97% and stability over 100 hours. In the all-solid-state reactor with DI water flow, pure formic acid solutions up to 2.85 M (~12.8 wt.%) were obtained with high selectivity. Using N2 gas flow, formic acid vapor was produced with concentrations up to nearly 100 wt.% after condensation, maintaining high selectivity (~72% at 11.0 wt.%).

The all-solid-state cell demonstrated stable continuous production of ~0.1 M pure formic acid solution for 100 hours at 30 mA cm−2 current density with DI water flow, and 30 hours continuous production of ~0.35 M pure formic acid product with humidified N2 gas flow.

The formic acid Faradaic efficiency dramatically decreased at high product concentrations within the solid electrolyte layer when using DI water flow due to increased reverse reaction rate and increased crossover to the anode. The AEM membrane degrades over time due to partial neutralization by CO2 gas, leading to decreased ionic conductivity and increased cell voltage, causing performance decay at high current densities.

Ir-based catalysts exhibit multifunctional catalytic activity for oxygen evolution reaction (OER), hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), and hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR); amorphous IrOx surfaces are highly active for OER, while metallic Ir surfaces are active for HER and HOR.

Under OER operation, crystalline IrNi/C-HT nanoparticles generate an atomically-thin IrNiOx layer that reversibly transforms into metallic IrNi at cathodic potentials, restoring high activity for HER and HOR; in contrast, IrNi/C-LT forms a thick amorphous IrNiOx layer that irreversibly converts and loses HER/HOR activity.
Fuel starvation experiments in a single PEM fuel cell were conducted by switching the anode gas from H2 to Ar at a current density of 100 mA cm-2 to induce voltage reversal conditions.

IrNi/C-HT catalysts exhibit high catalytic activity and remarkable reversibility for HER, HOR, and OER, maintaining performance after OER cycling, while IrNi/C-LT catalysts show decreased HER and HOR activity after OER due to irreversible oxide formation.

In fuel starvation tests, IrNi/C-HT showed only 9.2% performance degradation compared to 26.9% for IrNi/C-LT and 37.2% for commercial Pt/C, demonstrating superior durability and reversibility under real device conditions.

In reverse voltage experiments in a water electrolyzer, IrNi/C-HT maintained stable cell voltage after multiple polarity reversals, whereas IrNi/C-LT showed gradual voltage increase, indicating better reversibility and stability of IrNi/C-HT.

IrNi/C-LT catalysts with low crystallinity form thick amorphous IrNiOx layers under OER conditions that irreversibly convert and cause loss of HER and HOR activity, leading to performance degradation.

Voltage reversal and fuel starvation induce large positive potentials on hydrogen electrodes causing corrosion and degradation; commercial Pt/C catalysts suffer carbon corrosion and significant performance loss under these conditions.

The cell contains a Ni(OH)2/NiOOH mediator sandwiched by a CO2RR gas diffusion electrode (GDE) and a HOR GDE, to decouple CO2RR and HOR while eliminating sluggish oxygen catalysis. The cell operates in two alternating steps: Step 1 reduces CO2 to CO or formate at the CO2RR GDE while oxidizing Ni(OH)2 to NiOOH; Step 2 uses Ni electrode and HOR GDE to harvest energy by reducing NiOOH back to Ni(OH)2 and oxidizing H2.

The Ni(OH)2/NiOOH mediator is supported on Ni foam (2×2 cm2). The CO2RR GDE is 1×1 cm2, and the HOR GDE is Pt/C with 0.1 mg cm−2 Pt loading. A 1.5 cm thick PEEK frame separates the CO2RR GDE and the mediator, with a Hg/HgO reference electrode placed there. The mediator and HOR GDE are separated by a 130-μm-thick porous separator soaked in 1 M KOH.

Step 1: CO2 is electrochemically reduced to CO or formate at the cathode, while Ni(OH)2 is oxidized to NiOOH at the anode. Step 2: NiOOH is reduced back to Ni(OH)2 at the cathode, while H2 is oxidized to water at the anode, generating electricity. This alternating operation enables continuous CO2 reduction coupled with hydrogen oxidation.

The oxidation of Ni(OH)2 to NiOOH (NiOR) has much lower overpotential and faster kinetics than the oxygen evolution reaction (OER), as shown by cyclic voltammetry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. NiOR is a single-electron transfer process, whereas OER involves four-electron-proton transfer and gas evolution, causing sluggish kinetics and higher overpotential.

CO2RR catalysts: Zn nanosheets on Cu foam show high selectivity and current density for CO production, with Zn-Cu-500 sample exhibiting 85.8% Faradaic efficiency at -1.0 V vs RHE. Bi2O3 porous nanospheres are highly selective for formate production with up to 89.0% Faradaic efficiency at -0.65 V.

Both NiOR and NiRR of Ni(OH)2/NiOOH electrode, as well as the OER of Co3O4 on Ni foam (1 × 1 cm2) were studied in a three-electrode system. 1 M KOH solution was used as the electrolyte, a platinum plate (1 × 1 cm2) was used as the counter electrode, and a Hg/HgO electrode was used as the reference electrode. LSV and CV was performed at a scan rate of 1 mV s−1. The Tafel slope was calculated using the Tafel equation: η = b log(j) + a, where η, b, and j represent the overpotential, Tafel slope, and current density, respectively.

CO2 is fed to the cathode side for electrochemical reduction. H2 is fed to the anode side during Step 2 for hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR). Electrolytes used include 1 M KOH for mediator operation and 6 M KOH for HOR to minimize polarization losses.

The cell achieves high selectivity and stability for CO and formate production with Faradaic efficiencies up to 95.3% and stability over 100 hours at current densities of 50 mA cm−2 and voltages below 0.9 V. The cell voltage for CO2-to-CO conversion is as low as 0.87 V at 50 mA cm−2, significantly lower than conventional CO2RR cells.

The Ni(OH)2/NiOOH mediator enables replacement of OER by NiOR, reducing anodic overpotential from 330 mV to 40 mV at 20 mA cm−2, lowering overall cell voltage by 0.15-0.23 V. The system shows voltage efficiencies of 78.4% at 20 mA cm−2 and >95% voltage efficiency in Step 2 (HOR).

The cell shows stable operation over 100 hours with minimal voltage degradation (0.11 V for CO and 0.12 V for formate generation). The gradient functional layer on Zn GDE remains intact with no cracks or delamination after longevity tests.

The continuous-flow reactor configuration includes a proton shuttle that participates in reactions forming lithium nitride and ammonia, with protons generated through HOR on PtAu anode catalysts and transferred via protonated shuttle species.

The proton shuttle should contain functional groups capable of donating/accepting protons, have proper pKa in the electrolyte to balance protonation ability and minimize side reactions, form a functional SEI layer on the cathode, have stable deprotonated form, optimal diffusion rate, and compatibility with the Li-NRR system.

The PtAu/SSC anode electrode is prepared by electrodeposition using a 5 μm pore size stainless steel cloth as working electrode and Pt mesh as counter electrode, with a current density of -0.2 A/cm2 applied for 2 min to deposit high surface area PtAu structures.

The working electrode (PtAu/Ti) was obtained from electrodeposited PtAu on a titanium stub (ϕ = 5 mm, 0.196 cm2). To prepare the PtAu/Ti electrode, an electrolyte of 10 mM H2PtCl6·6H2O with 10 mM HAuCl4·3H2O in 3 M H2SO4 was used. A current density of -1 A/cm2 was applied for 2 min with 2500 rpm during electrodeposition.
DFT calculations considered PtAu(211), 8-Pt aggregate PtAu(211), and Pt/Au(111) facets for reaction energy calculations of HOR and ethanol, phenol oxidation on PtAu catalysts.

Protons are generated through hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) on PtAu anode catalysts and transferred via protonated proton shuttles to the cathode where they protonate lithium nitride to release ammonia.
Phenol (PhOH) as a proton shuttle exhibits the highest Faradaic efficiency (FE) of 72 ± 3% towards ammonia at an optimal concentration of 37 mM in the Li-NRR process, surpassing ethanol (EtOH).
Using lithium phenoxide (PhOLi) as the deprotonated form of phenol achieves a FE of 74 ± 2%, close to that of PhOH, indicating effective proton transfer capability.
The FE of ammonia depends on the concentration of phenol, with an optimal concentration of 37 mM yielding the highest FE.

Acetic acid as a proton shuttle results in lower FE (~22%) due to enhanced competitive hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), but mixing PhOLi with acetic acid improves FE to 31%, indicating proton-buffering capability of PhOLi.

Phenylacetylene (R-Ph) based ligands coordinate with Cu(I) ions forming metal-organic polymers (MOPs) with stable Cu^δ+ sites via strong σ-π dative bonding, enhancing structural stability and tunable electronic structure of copper sites.

Methoxyl substitution reduces energy difference in potential-determining step (CO2 to *COOH) by 0.23 eV, increasing reactivity toward CO2 reduction; stronger *CO adsorption on OMe-PhCu compared to F-PhCu which weakens *CO adsorption.

A proton exchange membrane (PEM) electrolyzer with hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) as anode was constructed, demonstrating lower cell voltage and good stability at 200 mA cm−2 while maintaining considerable methane Faradaic efficiency.

CO2 electroreduction performance evaluated in 0.1 M K2SO4 acidic system (pH=3±0.05) using a flow cell with CO2 flow rate controlled at 35 sccm; methane is primary product with Faradaic efficiency of 68.8% at 300 mA cm−2 for OMe-PhCu.
Single-pass carbon efficiency (SPCE) of OMe-PhCu increases to 24.1% at 3 mL min−1 CO2 flow rate at 500 mA cm−2 with methane FE of 38.9%.

OMe-PhCu exhibits methane Faradaic efficiency of 68.8% at 300 mA cm−2 and partial current density of 324.5 mA cm−2 in acidic electrolyte; performance slightly decreases with increased catalyst loading and more acidic electrolyte.

OMe-PhCu shows good electrochemical stability with no obvious changes in in situ Raman spectra over wide potential range; copper remains in +1 oxidation state with no metallic copper formation after CO2RR.

Catalyst inks were prepared by dispersing catalyst powder with PTFE emulsion, ethanol, and Nafion solution followed by ultrasonication, then sprayed onto gas diffusion layers with specific mass loadings (0.5 mg cm⁻²) to form electrodes (doc_0718_chunk_069041, Results and Discussion).

Interstitial carbon infusion modulates Pd soft acid strength, weakens Pd-O bond energy, promotes formation and desorption of HCOOH, suppresses CO poisoning and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), enhancing selectivity and stability for acidic CO2 reduction to formic acid (doc_0718_chunk_068981, Results and Discussion).

Proton exchange membrane (PEM) electrolyzer used for acidic CO2 electroreduction with PdCx catalyst, operating at high current density (1000 mA cm-2) and low cell voltage (1.8 V), demonstrating long-term stability (500 hours) and high Faradaic efficiency for HCOOH (doc_0718_chunk_068981, doc_0718_chunk_069012, doc_0718_chunk_069013, Results and Discussion).

Anodic oxygen evolution reaction replaced by Pt-Ru black-catalyzed hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) in PEM electrolyzer, reducing total voltage and avoiding harmful hydrogen peroxide generation, enhancing membrane durability (doc_0718_chunk_069012, Results and Discussion).

Acidic electrolyte (0.5 M K2SO4, pH ~3 adjusted by H2SO4) used for CO2 electroreduction, suppressing carbonate formation and enabling high current density HCOOH production (doc_0718_chunk_068982, Results and Discussion).

PdCx/CNT catalyst achieves >95% Faradaic efficiency for HCOOH at 1000 mA cm-2 current density with high stability (500 hours) in acidic PEM electrolyzer at 1.8 V cell voltage (doc_0718_chunk_068981, doc_0718_chunk_069012, doc_0718_chunk_069013, Results and Discussion).

PdCx/CNT exhibits lower Tafel slope and charge transfer resistance, higher electrochemical double layer capacitance, and enhanced CO2 binding strength compared to Pd/CNT, indicating improved intrinsic catalytic activity and kinetics (doc_0718_chunk_068999, doc_0718_chunk_069000, Results and Discussion).

PdCx/CNT catalyst suppresses HER and CO formation, enhancing selectivity and stability for HCOOH production, confirmed by electrochemical measurements, product analysis, and CO stripping tests (doc_0718_chunk_068993, doc_0718_chunk_068995, doc_0718_chunk_069001, doc_0718_chunk_069002, Results and Discussion).

Polarization curves were corrected for 95% iR drop with measured resistance of 1.1 ± 0.03 Ω; durability tests were performed by chronoamperometry in 0.5 M K2SO4 (pH 3 ± 0.03) (doc_0718_chunk_069039, Results and Discussion).
Faradaic efficiencies (FE) of liquid product HCOOH were calculated using ¹H NMR spectroscopy with internal standard DMSO and related equations (doc_0718_chunk_069044, Results and Discussion).

The proton-exchange membrane (PEM) system reduces CO2 to formic acid at the r-Pb catalyst, coupling CO2 reduction with hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) at the anode, achieving over 93% Faradaic efficiency and nearly 91% single-pass CO2 conversion efficiency at 600 mA cm-2 and 2.2 V cell voltage.

The PEM system uses a proton-exchange membrane (Nafion 212) and achieves durable operation for more than 5,200 hours at 2.2 V and 600 mA cm-2, with membrane stability attributed to the use of HOR at the anode to avoid harmful hydrogen peroxide generation that could degrade the membrane.

The cathode is fed with high-purity CO2 gas at controlled flow rates (optimized at 3 sccm for high single-pass conversion efficiency), and the anode is fed with humid hydrogen gas at 20 sccm for the hydrogen oxidation reaction.

The PEM electrolyser achieves a formic acid Faradaic efficiency over 93%, a current density up to 1.2 A cm-2 at 2.4 V, and a nearly 91% single-pass CO2 conversion efficiency at 600 mA cm-2 and 2.2 V cell voltage.

The system demonstrates excellent pH tolerance with high Faradaic efficiency across pH values, low carbon loss (<1%) in acid PEM system compared to 50% in alkaline electrolyte, and stable operation for over 5,200 hours with start-up/shut-down compatibility.
A scaled-up PEM reactor with 5x5 cm2 electrode area achieves 15 A current at roughly 2.7 V with over 91% Faradaic efficiency for formic acid generation, demonstrating scalability.

Electrolyte overflow induced by electrowetting of the three-phase interface can cause system failure; stability is maintained by reprocessing the gas diffusion electrode with PTFE emulsion every 200 hours or by using PTFE membrane-supported r-Pb catalysts for 2,000 hours without reprocessing.

Use of hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) at the anode instead of water oxidation reaction (WOR) avoids generation of harmful hydrogen peroxide that could degrade and destroy the proton-exchange membrane, contributing to membrane durability and system longevity.

GDLs require high conductivity for electron transfer, high porosity for gas diffusion, a smooth surface to accommodate the CL, and high water-repellence to prevent flooding; flooding blocks active catalytic sites and gas pathways, triggering severe hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and resulting in GDE performance failure.

Single-layer GDLs are macroporous substrates usually made of carbon material, with hydrophobic agent content (e.g., PTFE) determining surface hydrophobicity, gas permeability, and electrical conductivity; excessive PTFE can reduce hydrophobicity and increase electrical resistance.

Dual-layer GDLs include a microporous layer (MPL) between the macroporous layer and catalyst layer, improving hydrophobicity, water management, reducing ohmic resistance, and enhancing structural integrity; MPL is typically made of carbon black powder and hydrophobic agents like PTFE or PVDF.

Ink-based catalyst deposition methods include air-brushing, drop-casting, and hand-painting; air-brushing produces a thicker, more uniform CL with better performance and lower HER, while drop-casting and hand-painting can lead to thinner, uneven CLs and increased HER.

Electrodeposition comprises three main methods: constant current (galvanostatic), constant potential (potentiostatic), and pulse electrodeposition, where electrodeposition stops for a designated duration and then restarts. The catalytic performance with pulse electrodeposition is better than that with the other two methods. Electrodeposition also tunes the morphology of the catalyst layer (CL), altering the product selectivity. Sonication during electrodeposition results in better ion movement towards the gas diffusion layer (GDL), and uniform deposition.

Alternatively, the catalyst can be directly deposited on the ion-exchange membrane, and then a carbon-based GDL is inserted to the cell together with the MEA (membrane-based MEA fabrication). This method is more commonly used due to the superior catalyst layer/ion-exchange membrane interface. MEA is prone to swelling due to hydration during electrochemical reactions, and CL directly attached to the ion-exchange membrane shows better stability if swelling happens. Hot-pressing the membrane at around 120 °C improves the connection. Membrane-based fabrication shows superior performance in terms of reduced ohmic resistance and improved current density compared to GDL-based.

examples.section_title: 3.7. Recent advances in GDEs for CO2 reduction to hydrocarbons (methane, ethylene) and alcohols (ethanol, methanol, and propanol)
examples.value: Cu nanocubes rich with (110) facets have better C–C coupling to the intermediate *CO than (111) facets, enabling high activity for C2 products.

examples.section_title: 3.7. Recent advances in GDEs for CO2 reduction to hydrocarbons (methane, ethylene) and alcohols (ethanol, methanol, and propanol)
examples.value: A Cu/ZnO tandem GDE design feeds CO from ZnO to Cu layer, maintaining high CO concentration for further reduction to hydrocarbons, increasing CO utilization efficiency and improving performance.

examples.section_title: 4.2. Recent advances in GDEs for CORR
examples.value: Cu nanoparticles with open cavities promote C3 formation via nanoconfinement of C2 intermediates, leading to higher FE of propanol compared to solid nanoparticles or half-open cavities.

examples.section_title: 7.5. Development of GDEs with a new design/shape
examples.value: Hollow fiber gas diffusion electrodes (HFGDEs) have a tubular shape providing a much higher surface area compared with planar GDEs in a certain volume, enabling better performance than planar electrodes. Lung-inspired, pouch-like electrodes made of hydrophobic polyethylene membrane sputtered with gold offer abundant access to catalytic active sites at the three-phase interface and enable local pH tuning to suppress HER.
Epitaxial rutile IrO2 and RuO2 films with (100) orientation have greater OER activity than (110) orientation, possibly due to enhanced active site density.
(110) orientation in Co3O4 nanocrystals contains more active Co3+ phase and features greater OER activity than (001) plane.
SrRuO3 single-crystal thin films show OER activity decreasing in order (111) > (110) > (001), with stability increasing in same order, indicating a stability-activity relation.
OER activity of RuO2 and Co3O4 metal-based catalysts increases upon decreasing particle size, though explanations vary among studies.
Ru nanoparticles of 4±1 nm diameter completely dissolve during first anodic scan, indicating poor stability of nanoscale catalysts.
Incorporation of Ni or Co into ruthenia lowers *OH and *OOH adsorption energies, enhancing OER activity; however, experimental effects are less significant than predicted.
Leaching of Ni atoms from bimetallic Ir–Ni oxide surface during OER promotes formation of reactive surface hydroxyl groups, a descriptor for OER activity.
Ni(OH)2/NiOOH redox transitions involve multiple phases with different oxidation states and hydration, affecting OER activity and surface properties.
Fe-doped NiO shows higher OER activity than pure NiO; active metal phase is Fe, with Fe3+ incorporation optimizing adsorption energy of reaction intermediates.

Perovskite oxides ABO3 have tunable electrocatalytic activity via cation substitution at A and B sites, but surface oxidation state identification is difficult due to large particle size and bulk characterization limitations.

Direct CO reduction to formaldehyde (CORTF) is achieved under ambient conditions using MoP catalyst, combining electrochemical and thermal mechanisms. The reaction involves hydrogen underpotential deposition (HUPD) generating *H species that react with dissolved CO to form formaldehyde with nearly 100% Faradaic efficiency at low current density.
CO is fed to the cathode compartment where CORTF occurs, and H2 is fed to the anode compartment for hydrogen oxidation reaction in the full cell setup.

Formaldehyde production rate reaches over 30 mg/(g_cat h) with more than 50% Faradaic efficiency by tuning current density and temperature, which is one order of magnitude higher than previous thermal catalysis methods. Faradaic efficiency approaches 96.6% at low current density (-5 μA cm-2).

The reaction shows hybrid electrochemical and thermal kinetics with apparent activation energy of 17.1 kJ/mol. Increasing KOH concentration enhances formaldehyde production rate due to improved hydrogen deposition activity.

In full cell configuration, formaldehyde is produced spontaneously at zero cell voltage at a rate of 6.1 mg/(g_cat h), with increased rate at negative cell voltages. The full cell shows good durability with less than 5 μA cm-2 current decay over 24 h.

The MoP catalyst shows good stability during 24 h durability test at 0.2 V vs RHE with less than 20 μA cm-2 current density decrease. Slight rate decrease is attributed to reduced CO mass transfer due to low CO solubility. XRD after stability test shows no structural changes.

Hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) in alkaline electrolyte follows Tafel-Volmer or Heyrovsky-Volmer mechanisms with key intermediate adsorbed hydrogen (Had); elementary steps include H2 + 2* -> 2Had (Tafel), H2 + OH- + * -> Had + H2O + e- (Heyrovsky), Had + OH- -> H2O + e- + * (Volmer).

Ni/N-CNT catalyst shows HOR activity similar to platinum-group metals in alkaline electrolyte; nitrogen-doped carbon nanotubes alone are poor HOR catalysts but as support increase Ni nanoparticle catalytic performance by factor of 33 (mass activity) and 21 (exchange current density) relative to unsupported Ni nanoparticles.
Hydroxide exchange membrane fuel cells (HEMFCs) operate in alkaline environment enabling use of PGM-free catalysts such as Ni/N-CNT for HOR at anode.

Electrochemical HOR performance tested in 0.1 M KOH solution using rotating disk electrode with Ni loading of 0.25 mg cm-2; H2-saturated electrolyte used for polarization curves; Ar-saturated electrolyte used as control showing no anodic current.

Ni/N-CNT catalyst shows much higher HOR current density and lower onset potential (~0 V vs RHE) than Ni/CNT or unsupported Ni; mass activity of Ni/N-CNT is 9.3 mA mgNi-1 at 50 mV overpotential, 33 times higher than unsupported Ni; exchange current density of Ni/N-CNT is 0.028 mA cmNi-2, 21 times higher than unsupported Ni and comparable to some PGM catalysts like Pd.

Electrochemical surface area (ECSA) normalized kinetic currents show Ni/N-CNT has higher intrinsic activity; charge transfer coefficient a between 0.4 and 0.5 indicating good symmetry for HOR and HER branches.

Theoretical calculations predict order of magnitude increase in exchange current density for graphene-supported Ni nanocluster compared to unsupported Ni; further improvement with nitrogen doping at nanoparticle edge; Ni/N-CNT stabilization and activation attributed to geometric and electronic effects of nitrogen dopants.

Hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) at the anode is challenged by CO impurities in reformate hydrogen, which deactivate Pt catalysts; Ru cores with Pt shells promote preferential CO oxidation and improve CO tolerance.
Pt shell thickness affects CO binding energy and oxygen adsorption behavior, influencing CO tolerance and HOR activity; bilayer Pt shells provide an optimal balance.
CO oxidation on Ru@Pt nanoparticles is preferential compared to Ru–Pt alloys and other Pt shell/core combinations, enhancing CO tolerance in hydrogen feeds.
Proton exchange membrane (PEM) fuel cells require catalysts with high activity and durability, minimizing precious metal use; Ru@Pt core-shell nanoparticles are designed for PEM fuel cell anodes.

Accelerated stress tests cycling anode potential between 0.02 and 0.95 V show little change in performance for Ru@Pt catalysts, indicating excellent corrosion resistance of Pt shells protecting Ru cores.
Hydrogen feed containing ppm-level CO impurities severely deactivates Pt catalysts; preferential oxidation reduces CO to about 10 ppm, motivating development of CO-tolerant catalysts.

Use of inexpensive reformate hydrogen with CO impurities is enabled by Ru@Pt catalysts with bilayer Pt shells, maintaining performance with less than half reduction in current at 0.65 V compared to pure hydrogen.
Ru@Pt core-shell catalysts with bilayer Pt shells exhibit doubled current density at 0.65 V under CO-containing hydrogen compared to Pt/C baseline, demonstrating enhanced CO tolerance.
Bilayer Ru@Pt nanoparticles show superior durability and CO tolerance at ultra-low catalyst loadings (25 μg cm⁻² Pt, 10 μg cm⁻² Ru) in PEM fuel cells.
Lattice contractions in Pt shells (-0.6 to -1.0%) weaken CO adsorption and contribute to CO tolerance, but must be balanced to maintain HOR activity and oxidative CO removal.

examples.section_title: 7 Pacific Northwest National Laboratory,902 Battelle Blvd, Richland,Washington 99352,USA
examples.value: Microstructure optimization of nickel-cermet anodes has relied on empirical improvement of materials specifications to control cermet morphology. Modern submicrometre active ceramic powder allows sintering temperatures to be decreased to 1400 °C or lower, with lower metal contents. Nickel oxide grain size around 1 µm is used, with a bidispersed ceramic component containing coarse powder grains of 25 µm or larger to form the structural skeleton and inhibit nickel aggregation, mixed with fine powder to promote sintering. This is applied to electrolyte-supported configurations with stabilized zirconia substrates 150 µm or thicker, providing structural integrity. Recent developments extend the a

examples.section_title: 7 Pacific Northwest National Laboratory,902 Battelle Blvd, Richland,Washington 99352,USA
examples.value: In the Cu/CeO system, a porous skeleton is first obtained by ceramic methods and then the Cu and ceria are added in subsequent, low temperature steps, resulting in very advantageous microstructure and improved thermal stability.

examples.section_title: 7 Pacific Northwest National Laboratory,902 Battelle Blvd, Richland,Washington 99352,USA
examples.value: In Ni/YSZ cermet anodes, nickel acts as catalyst for hydrogen oxidation and steam reforming of methane, producing a hydrogen-rich synthesis gas that undergoes electrochemical oxidation at the three-phase boundary. Internal reforming SOFCs generally operate above 900 °C due to thermodynamic limitations of steam reforming. Nickel also catalyzes carbon filament formation from hydrocarbons under reducing conditions, which can destroy the anode unless sufficient steam is present to remove carbon faster than it forms. High steam-to-carbon ratios are needed to suppress carbon deposition with methane, but this approach does not work for higher hydrocarbons, requiring pre-reforming with steam or oxyg

examples.section_title: 7 Pacific Northwest National Laboratory,902 Battelle Blvd, Richland,Washington 99352,USA
examples.value: Alternative anode materials focus on catalytic activity for oxidation of methane or higher hydrocarbons and inactivity for cracking reactions leading to carbon deposition. Metals like Cu, Ag, and Au are less active for cracking but not highly active oxidation catalysts. Ceramic anodes with electronic or mixed electronic–ionic conductivity have been developed, chosen mainly for conductivity rather than catalytic properties. Composite ceramic anodes are suggested to optimize transport and catalytic properties. Direct hydrocarbon operation anodes have lower performance than Ni/YSZ anodes on hydrogen, indicating room for improvement in catalytic properties and need for understanding surface reac

examples.section_title: 7 Pacific Northwest National Laboratory,902 Battelle Blvd, Richland,Washington 99352,USA
examples.value: More basic work to probe the mechanistic details of fuel utilisation reactions needs to be undertaken to rationally design more efficient anodes and thus high-performance SOFCs powered by practical fuels.

examples.section_title: 7 Pacific Northwest National Laboratory,902 Battelle Blvd, Richland,Washington 99352,USA
examples.value: The electrolyte in SOFCs is normally yttria-stabilized zirconia (YSZ), which offers good oxide ion transport while blocking electronic transport. Its function is to allow transport of oxide ions from the air electrode interface to the fuel electrode interface. The electrolyte substrate can be 150 µm or thicker for structural integrity, or as thin as 10 µm when supported by the anode material, permitting lower operating temperatures and improved durability.

examples.section_title: 7 Pacific Northwest National Laboratory,902 Battelle Blvd, Richland,Washington 99352,USA
examples.value: SOFCs can use a diverse range of fuels including hydrogen, methane, diesel, coal, liquid hydrocarbons (propane, butane, gasoline, diesel, JP-8), alcohols (methanol, ethanol), dimethyl ether, ammonia, and coal gasification products. Natural gas methane with small amounts of other hydrocarbons is widely used, often with partial external reforming. Bio-derived fuels like landfill gas contain impurities such as ammonia, hydrogen sulphide, and halogens. Sulphur-containing impurities can degrade anode performance if not removed. Liquid hydrocarbons have high energy densities but contain sulphur impurities causing coking and sulphur contamination. Alcohols are considered for portable applications b

examples.section_title: 7 Pacific Northwest National Laboratory,902 Battelle Blvd, Richland,Washington 99352,USA
examples.value: Nickel-cermet anodes provide high current densities operating on hydrogen but have limitations with hydrocarbons due to carbon deposition. Copper-ceria-YSZ composite anodes show effective direct use of various hydrocarbons including butane and decane, with high resistance to carbon deposition. SOFCs with Cu/CeO2/YSZ anodes exhibit higher current densities on hydrogen than hydrocarbons, indicating catalysis plays a critical role and catalytic activity enhancements are needed for hydrocarbon fuels.

examples.section_title: 7 Pacific Northwest National Laboratory,902 Battelle Blvd, Richland,Washington 99352,USA
examples.value: Reasonable performance with direct methane use has been achieved with electronically conductive ceramic anodes such as La-doped strontium titanate and Gd-doped ceria. Addition of ceria enhances performance significantly. Mn-doped LaCrO with YSZ and small amounts of Ni or Ru substitution also shows stable power generation with methane and propane. Split B-site perovskites like (La,Sr)Mn0.5Cr0.5O3 provide good performance and chemical stability.

examples.section_title: 7 Pacific Northwest National Laboratory,902 Battelle Blvd, Richland,Washington 99352,USA
examples.value: New fuel electrode formulations for second-generation commercial fuel cells can offer improved redox tolerance and better resistance stability in hydrocarbon fuels.

Hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) on Pt(111) is extremely fast and requires only a small number of Pt sites; calix[4]arene modification preserves Pt-like HOR activity even at high coverage (doc_1216_chunk_111457)

examples.section_title: RRDE method electrolytes and electrochemical set-up
examples.value: Experiments conducted in 0.1 M HClO4 electrolyte with controlled gas purging (Ar, O2, H2) to study HOR and ORR on Pt and Pt-calix surfaces (doc_1216_chunk_111473, doc_1216_chunk_111474)

Pt(111)-calix surfaces show high selectivity for HOR over ORR, with only 2% of Pt sites available sufficient to reach diffusion limiting currents for HOR while strongly inhibiting ORR (doc_1216_chunk_111457, doc_1216_chunk_111459, doc_1216_chunk_111466)

Cathode degradation during shutdown/startup in PEMFCs is linked to undesired ORR on anode side; selective anode catalysts that suppress ORR while preserving HOR activity are needed (doc_1216_chunk_111441, doc_1216_chunk_111442)

DFT calculations show CoN4C12, CoN3C10,porp, and CoN2C5 moieties have Co(II) binding energies of −6.8 to −7.5 eV and O2 adsorption energies from −0.80 to −1.26 eV, indicating stable porphyrinic structures with relatively weak O2 binding compared to Fe moieties.
Co-based moieties bind O2 too weakly for efficient oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) compared to Fe-based moieties, as supported by DFT and experimental redox potentials.
Operando XANES shows Co moieties do not change oxidation state from 0.0 to 1.0 V vs. RHE during ORR, unlike Fe moieties which undergo structural and electronic changes.

Co moieties exhibit activity for oxygen evolution reaction (OER) and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) in acid medium, but low stability limits practical OER application; no activity observed for hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR).
ORR activity and selectivity of Co0.5 catalyst measured in acidic medium (0.1 M H2SO4) using rotating ring disk electrode (RRDE) with O2-saturated electrolyte.
Operando XANES spectra recorded in O2- and N2-saturated acidic electrolyte to study adsorption and redox behavior of Co and Fe moieties during ORR.
Co0.5 catalyst shows ORR current density of 0.9 mA cm−2 at 0.8 V vs. RHE with 3–5% peroxide selectivity at 800 µg cm−2 loading, comparable to state-of-the-art Co–N–C catalysts in acid.
In PEM fuel cell tests, Co0.5 shows lower ORR activity than Fe0.5 but lower apparent Tafel slope at high potential, leading to higher power performance below 0.55 V.
Polarization curves were recorded by scanning the cell voltage at 0.5 mV s−1 during PEM fuel cell tests with a single-cell fuel cell with serpentine flow field.
Low stability of carbon support at high potentials limits practical application of Co–N–C catalysts for OER in acid medium due to carbon corrosion.

Ni N/Ni interfacial electrocatalysts were synthesized through cathodic electrodeposition of porous Ni microspheres on current collectors such as Ni foam (NF) or carbon foam (CF), followed by thermal nitridation in ammonia to create rich Ni3N/Ni interfacial sites. The nitridation temperature was optimized to 300 °C for 6 h to obtain the best HER activity. The resultant Ni3N/Ni/NF possessed a 3D macroporous ligament network structure with stacked coarse particles over the skeleton surface, and XRD, HRTEM, and XPS confirmed the coexistence and interface between hexagonal Ni3N and cubic Ni phases.

Ni3N/Ni/NF catalyzes hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) starting at nearly zero overpotential, with robust long-term durability and unity Faradaic efficiency. The interfacial sites between Ni3N and Ni provide appropriate hydrogen adsorption free energy near zero, facilitating both HER and HOR. The interface also promotes water adsorption and lowers the activation energy barrier for water dissociation, critical for HER in neutral and alkaline electrolytes.

The Ni3N/Ni/NF electrocatalyst shows exceptional HER performance with overpotentials of only 19 mV and 12 mV to deliver −10 mA cm−2 current density in 1.0 M potassium phosphate buffer (pH 7.17) and 1.0 M KOH (pH 13.80), respectively, outperforming Pt/C under similar conditions. It also exhibits superior HOR activity in 0.1 M KOH with great tolerance to CO poisoning, maintaining high current density and stability over 24 h.

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations show that hydrogen atoms preferentially adsorb at the Ni3N/Ni interface with free energy changes of hydrogen adsorption (ΔG_H*) close to zero (0.01 eV and −0.07 eV), indicating optimal binding for HER/HOR. The interface also facilitates water adsorption and reduces the activation energy barrier for water dissociation (0.50 eV), lower than on pure Ni or Ni3N surfaces, supporting the experimentally observed high activity.

In a small potential window of the micro-polarization region near the equilibrium potential (±20 mV vs. RHE), the current i approximately equals the measured current. The Butler–Volmer equation can be expanded by Taylor’s formula and simplified as i = i0 (η F)/(R T). The exchange current density (j) was calculated by dividing i0 by the geometric electrode area.

Hydroxide exchange membrane fuel cells (HEMFCs) operate in alkaline electrolytes, enabling the use of inexpensive oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) electrocatalysts. However, the hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) in alkaline media is kinetically challenging, even for Pt, with HOR performance two orders of magnitude lower than in acidic proton exchange membrane fuel cells (PEMFCs). Developing Earth-abundant electrocatalysts with high HOR activity and CO tolerance is critical for HEMFCs.

The electrocatalytic performance of Ni3N/Ni/NF was evaluated in aqueous electrolytes saturated with hydrogen gas (H2). HER was tested in 1.0 M potassium phosphate buffer (pH 7.17) and 1.0 M KOH (pH 13.80). HOR was measured in H2-saturated 0.1 M KOH with continuous H2 bubbling to mimic hydroxide exchange membrane fuel cell (HEMFC) conditions. CO tolerance was tested using a H2 gas mixture containing 2% CO (v/v).

Ni3N/Ni/NF exhibits extraordinary HER activity with nearly zero onset overpotential and requires only 12 to 19 mV overpotential to achieve −10 mA cm−2 current density in neutral and alkaline electrolytes, outperforming Pt/C catalysts under similar conditions. It maintains stable potential over 50 h of galvanostatic electrolysis and shows negligible degradation after 5000 to 10,000 cyclic voltammetry cycles. The Faradaic efficiency is close to 100%.

For HOR in 0.1 M KOH, Ni3N/Ni/NF shows onset potential near 0 V vs. RHE and achieves higher current density than Pt/C at increasing potentials. The exchange current density is 3.08 mA cm−2, 1.4 times that of Pt/NF. It maintains stable anodic current density (~8 mA cm−2) for 24 h under H2 saturation. Ni3N/Ni/NF also demonstrates exceptional CO tolerance, with only a 3.5% decrease in HOR current density in 2% CO-containing H2, whereas Pt/NF suffers ~63% decrease.
The exchange current density (j) was calculated by dividing i0 by the geometric electrode area, where i0 can be obtained from the slope of the linear fitting in the micropolarization region.

Surface morphology of HfNxOy films after electrochemical testing showed features similar to the Au substrate particles with a surface roughness factor of 1.5 by AFM and 2.25 by electrochemical double-layer measurements, indicating low surface roughness and that catalytic performance was not mainly from increased surface area (chunk_id=doc_1283_chunk_116366, section_title=Results).

HfNxOy thin films exhibit near-zero onset potentials for both hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) in acidic media, with large cathodic current for HER and anodic current for HOR, similar to platinum group metals (chunk_id=doc_1283_chunk_116351, section_title=Results).

The HER overpotential at 10 mA cm^-2 for HfNxOy thin film is -75 mV vs RHE, only 35 mV more negative than Pt foil; exchange current density for HER reached 0.40 mA cm^-2 (29 mA per mg Hf); Tafel slope was 72 mV dec^-1 indicating a different reaction mechanism than Pt (27 mV dec^-1) (chunk_id=doc_1283_chunk_116355, section_title=Results).

HfNxOy thin film demonstrates substantial HOR activity under acidic conditions, reaching H2 diffusion limited current at an overpotential of 50 mV, only 20 mV more positive than Pt foil (chunk_id=doc_1283_chunk_116355, section_title=Results).

Electrochemical polarization scans were carried out in H2-purged 0.1 M HClO4 solutions to measure HER and HOR activity and define zero potential on the RHE scale (chunk_id=doc_1283_chunk_116354, section_title=Results).

HfNxOy thin film exhibits HER and HOR catalytic activity approaching that of Pt foil under acidic conditions, with zero onset potential for both reactions and high current densities (chunk_id=doc_1283_chunk_116357, section_title=Results).

The HER overpotential at 10 mA cm^-2 is -75 mV vs RHE, and the exchange current density is 0.40 mA cm^-2; HOR current reaches diffusion limit at 50 mV overpotential (chunk_id=doc_1283_chunk_116355, section_title=Results).

HfNxOy thin films demonstrate high chemical and physical durability in strong acidic media, maintaining electrochemical stability after 1000 cyclic voltammetry scans between -300 and 1500 mV vs RHE; surface morphology and elemental distribution remain unchanged after testing (chunk_id=doc_1283_chunk_116366, section_title=Results).

There are four types of electrochemical coproduction: parallel, convergent, divergent, and linear paired electrolysis. Parallel paired electrolysis features simultaneous occurrence of two unrelated half-reactions in a divided cell. Convergent paired electrolysis produces a single product from intermediates formed in cathode and anode in an undivided cell. Divergent paired electrolysis uses a common starting substrate in both electrodes, leading to different products. Linear paired electrolysis produces the same product from the same substrate via different electrochemical reactions.

The electrolyzer bare module cost accounts for approximately 50–75% of capital expenditures (CAPEX), with 41% of this cost from stack systems and about 60% from catalyst and membrane costs. Other significant CAPEX contributors include PSA systems and distillation systems. Operating expenditures (OPEX) are dominated by feed costs, with 13–56% for electrolyte make-up and 0–53% for organic raw materials. Electricity costs account for 3–25% of OPEX. Therefore, critical elements for potential products include high product recovery, long-term catalyst stability, low electrolyte and raw material costs, low cell potential, and high mass production rate with low electrons required per unit molecular

16 cathodic reactions including CO2RR and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) were selected and divided into three categories based on Faraday efficiency, partial current density, and cell potential. 18 anodic reactions including OOR and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) were also divided into three categories based on raw material source. A commercial electrolyzer with parallel paired electrolysis was considered for analysis.

Half-cell reaction information includes number of required electrons (z), standard reduction potential, and overpotential, arranged in supplementary tables, used for techno-economic analysis (doc_1310_chunk_118556, Discussion).

Global sensitivity analysis (GSA) using Fourier amplitude sensitivity testing (FAST) was performed to quantify effects of current density, Faraday efficiency (FE), and overpotential on levelized cost of chemicals (LCC). The analysis showed that LCCs of CO2RR–OER products are highly sensitive to FE when LCC is high, with current density having sharp effect at low values but gradual effect at higher values. LCC of OOR products is sensitive to both current density and FE due to higher economic competitiveness and hydrogen sales margin. Sensitivity depends on market prices of products and raw materials.

At very low performance levels (low current density and FE), technological advancements yield rapid changes in LCC, but effects decrease after certain technology maturity is achieved. High current density has less impact on lowering LCC beyond certain levels. FE is generally key factor due to OPEX dominance, but at low current density CAPEX becomes significant. Economies of scale affect LCC sensitivity boundaries.

Solar-to-chemical efficiency (STC) and unit CO2 conversion at the electrolyzer are defined mathematically using standard reduction potentials, current density, Faradaic efficiency, and photovoltaic energy input. Equations (9) to (13) describe production rates, electrolyzer area, CO2 conversion, and STC (doc_1310_chunk_118570 to doc_1310_chunk_118577, Discussion).

The cathode and anode were separated by a porous solid electrolyte (PSE) layer, where electrochemically generated formate and proton were recombined to form molecular formic acid. Gas diffusion layer (GDL) electrodes coated with CO2RR and HOR catalysts were used as cathode and anode respectively, with anion and cation exchange membranes (AEM and CEM) for efficient ion transportation.

CO2 is selectively reduced to formate ions on the cathode catalyst; on the anode side, hydrogen gas is oxidized (HOR) to release protons. Electrochemically generated HCOO- and H+ are driven by electric field across AEM and CEM into the PSE layer, where they recombine to form formic acid molecules.

The nBuLi-Bi catalyst achieved high formate partial current densities (>440 mA cm-2) with maximal Faradaic efficiency ~97% and stability over 100 hours. The catalyst showed improved activity compared to oxide-derived Bi due to grain boundaries and increased surface area.

The cathode side was supplied with humidified CO2 gas, and the anode side was supplied with humidified H2 gas for the hydrogen oxidation reaction. An inert gas (N2) flow was used through the PSE layer to carry away formic acid vapors for high-concentration product collection.

Using an inert N2 gas flow instead of DI water flow to discharge formic acid vapors avoids product accumulation in the PSE layer, maintaining high Faradaic efficiency and enabling high-concentration formic acid collection by cold condensation.

The all-solid-state reactor achieved ultra-high concentrations of pure formic acid solutions up to nearly 100 wt.% condensed from generated vapors by tuning the carrier gas stream. The nBuLi-Bi catalyst showed formate partial current densities >440 mA cm-2, maximal Faradaic efficiency ~97%, and stability over 100 hours.

Using DI water flow through the PSE layer, pure formic acid solutions up to 2.85 M (~12.8 wt.%) were obtained by lowering water flow rate, with formic acid Faradaic efficiency above 80% at current densities up to 421 mA cm-2. Stability of 100 hours continuous production at ~0.1 M formic acid was demonstrated.

Using humidified N2 gas flow to carry formic acid vapor, condensed formic acid solutions up to 60.5 wt.% (14.8 M) were obtained at low N2 flow rates, maintaining ~72% Faradaic efficiency. Using dry N2 flow, nearly 100 wt.% pure formic acid was obtained directly without downstream purification.

At high current densities, formic acid selectivity decreased possibly due to CO2 mass transport limitations and increased hydrogen evolution side reaction. Stability degradation at high current density was mainly caused by degradation of the anion exchange membrane (AEM) due to partial neutralization by CO2 gas, leading to decreased ionic conductivity and increased cell voltage.

The MoNi4 and WNi4 catalysts catalyze the hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) efficiently in alkaline electrolytes, showing onset potentials near 0 V vs RHE and reaching diffusion-limiting currents at potentials >0.05 V. MoNi4 outperforms commercial Pt/C catalyst with a half-wave potential 5 mV lower. The exchange current density j0 for MoNi4 is 3.41 mA cm−2, 1.4 times higher than Pt/C, and WNi4 shows 1.87 mA cm−2. The catalysts exhibit low activation energies (18.59 kJ/mol for MoNi4) and high intrinsic activities normalized by electrochemical surface area.

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations show that alloying Ni with Mo or W weakens the hydrogen binding energy (HBE) on Ni sites and enhances hydroxyl binding energy (OHBE) on Mo(W) sites, synergistically promoting the Volmer step in alkaline HOR. Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) and CO-stripping experiments support optimized adsorption of hydrogen and hydroxyl species on the alloys, explaining their superior HOR activity compared to pure Ni and Pt.

The HOR activity was tested in H2-saturated 0.1 M KOH alkaline electrolyte using a three-electrode setup with a rotating disk electrode (RDE). Catalyst loading was optimized at 0.5 mg cm−2 on glassy carbon electrode. CO tolerance was evaluated by introducing 20,000 ppm CO in the hydrogen feed, showing MoNi4 catalyst maintains HOR activity while Pt/C is poisoned.

The MoNi4 catalyst exhibits a geometric exchange current density of 3.41 mA cm−2 and a kinetic current density of 33.8 mA cm−2 at 50 mV overpotential, outperforming commercial Pt/C and freshly synthesized Ni catalysts. The activation energy for HOR on MoNi4 is 18.59 kJ mol−1, lower than Pt/C (20.07 kJ mol−1). The catalysts show excellent electrochemical stability with minimal current loss after 2000 cycles and stable chronoamperometry over 20 hours, outperforming Pt/C which degrades significantly.

The Pt/C catalyst suffers marked degradation during 20 h operation, retaining only 57% of original current density due to Pt nanoparticle agglomeration. In contrast, MoNi4 and WNi4 catalysts show negligible degradation and maintain morphology and structure after long-term testing.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis shows that after calcination, nanoparticles are partially oxidized. After HOR test, Ru is reduced to metallic form, while Ni remains predominantly in oxidized Ni(II) state on the surface, indicating a surface Ni oxide layer with interior RuNi alloy.

The hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) on Ru7Ni3/C involves H2 adsorption on catalyst surface forming adsorbed hydrogen (H*), followed by electron transfer and desorption as H+. The intrinsic hydrogen binding energy (HBE) between H* and metal is critical for HOR activity. Alloying Ru with Ni weakens the Ru-H binding strength, as shown by a shift in Ru-H bands to lower wavenumber in in situ ATR-SEIRAS, indicating weaker HBE. Enhanced water adsorption due to surface Ni oxides also contributes to optimizing apparent hydrogen binding energy (HBEapp), improving HOR activity.

The apparent hydrogen binding energy (HBEapp) is the net result of catalyst hydrogen binding and water adsorption. Ru7Ni3/C shows the lowest Hupd peak potential among Ru-containing catalysts, indicating HBEapp closest to zero and highest HOR activity. Enhanced water adsorption is confirmed by in situ ATR-SEIRAS showing stronger interaction of water molecules with Ru7Ni3/C, attributed to surface Ni oxides which are more hydrophilic than metals. Acid washing removing surface Ni oxides reduces HOR activity and water adsorption strength, confirming the critical role of surface Ni oxides.

The hydroxide exchange membrane used is PAP-TP-85, which can afford high operating temperature of 95 °C, beneficial for ultrahigh HEMFC performance. The membrane electrode assembly (MEA) was fabricated using Ru7Ni3/C as anode catalyst, commercial Pt/C as cathode, and PAP-TP-85 membrane.

Ru7Ni3/C catalyst exhibits high HOR activity with mass activity about 21 times that of Pt/C and 3 times that of PtRu/C, and specific activity about 25 times that of Pt/C and 5 times that of PtRu/C in rotating disk electrode (RDE) tests. The HEMFC with Ru7Ni3/C anode delivers a peak power density of 2.03 W cm−2 in H2/O2 and 1.23 W cm−2 in H2/air (CO2-free) at 95 °C, surpassing PtRu/C anode catalyst. The anode catalyst cost is reduced by 85% compared to PtRu/C. Durability test shows less than 5% voltage loss over 100 h operation at 500 mA cm−2 under H2/air (CO2-free).

The Ru7Ni3/C-based HEMFC shows excellent performance with current density of 2.0 A cm−2 at 0.65 V in H2/O2, higher than PtRu/C-based HEMFC (1.68 A cm−2). The catalyst loading is 0.2 mg Ru cm−2, lower than 0.6 mg PtRu cm−2 needed for similar performance. The catalyst also shows good durability with only 4.4% voltage loss after 100 h at 0.5 A cm−2 under H2/air (CO2-free).

The Ru7Ni3/C-based HEMFC shows good durability with less than 5% voltage loss over 100 h operation at 500 mA cm−2 under H2/air (CO2-free), indicating no significant failure or degradation during this period.

Ir exhibits excellent catalytic activity for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR), but irreversibly converts to amorphous IrO at potentials > 0.8 V/RHE, which is excellent for oxygen evolution reaction (OER) but poor for HER and HOR. The crystalline IrNi/C-HT catalyst generates an atomically-thin IrO layer under OER operation, which reversibly transforms into metallic Ir at more cathodic potentials, restoring high activity for HER and HOR.

IrNi/C-LT irreversibly converts to an amorphous IrNiOx surface after OER, leading to decreased HER and HOR performance. IrNi/C-HT possesses a very thin IrNiOx layer under OER conditions that reversibly converts to a metallic surface under HER conditions, exhibiting high HER and HOR performance.

The reversible catalytic activity of IrNi/C-HT is due to a dissolution and re-deposition mechanism where the metallic IrNi alloy under the thin IrOx layer acts as a catalytic substrate for reduction of dissolved Ir ions under HER conditions, enabling reversible conversion between oxide and metallic states.

Polymer electrolyte membrane (PEM) fuel cell operation suffers from fuel starvation at the anode side leading to voltage reversal and corrosion of carbon components such as catalyst support, gas diffusion layer (GDL), and flow field plate. Applying reversible multifunctional electrocatalysts like crystalline IrNi/C-HT to the hydrogen electrode can mitigate degradation by maintaining catalytic activity during voltage reversal.

In fuel starvation experiments in a single PEM fuel cell, the anode gas was switched from H2 to Ar at a current density of 100 mA cm-2 to induce fuel starvation and voltage reversal conditions. In water electrolyzer reverse voltage experiments, cell polarity was changed every 20 min at a constant current density of 40 mA cm-2 to test catalyst reversibility under intermittent switching between HER and OER.

IrNi/C-HT and IrNi/C-LT catalysts exhibit excellent OER, HER, and HOR catalytic activity. IrNi/C-HT maintains high HER and HOR activity after OER cycling due to reversible surface oxide formation, whereas IrNi/C-LT shows decreased HER and HOR activity due to irreversible oxide formation. In PEM fuel cell fuel starvation tests, IrNi/C-HT showed only 9.2% performance degradation compared to 26.9% for IrNi/C-LT and 37.2% for commercial Pt/C. In water electrolyzer reverse voltage tests, IrNi/C-HT maintained stable cell voltage after multiple polarity reversals, unlike IrNi/C-LT which showed gradual voltage increase.

Voltage reversal in water electrolyzers and fuel starvation in PEM fuel cells induce large positive potentials on hydrogen electrodes, causing severe system degradation including corrosion of carbon components and catalyst degradation. IrNi/C-LT with low crystallinity forms thick amorphous IrNiOx layers that are irreversible and lead to poor HER and HOR performance after OER, indicating failure due to irreversible oxidation. Carbon corrosion was observed in commercial Pt/C catalysts under fuel starvation but not in IrNi/C catalysts.

Bimetallic PtM catalysts alloy Pt with lanthanides or 3d transition metals to create compressive strain in the Pt enriched shell, improving ORR activity by tuning oxygen adsorption energy (doc_1424_chunk_128130).
Conformally deposited ultrathin Pt shells improve durability of Pt catalysts by ligand effects and electron interactions, with shell thickness being critical (doc_1424_chunk_128132).
Thermal annealing of PtM alloys combined with acid leaching is used to achieve desired surface structure and stable composition profiles (doc_1424_chunk_128132).

Higher loading of PGM-free catalysts is needed to achieve comparable performance compared to PGM-based catalysts due to lower intrinsic ORR activity, causing thicker catalyst layers (CL). The CL structure is influenced by pore-size distribution of catalyst, ionomer loading and distribution, and deposition process, affecting ionomer infiltration and distribution, local microstructural transport resistance, and ionic conductivity within the CL. Water produced during PEMFC operation can block active sites or inhibit mass transport when pooling at interfaces. Optimizing CL wettability can alter water accumulation and transport to avoid pore blockage. Non-uniform drying can cause cracks and voids
Atomically dispersed Fe-N4 moieties possess the highest four-electron ORR activity among identified active sites in Fe–N–C catalysts due to unique electronic properties (doc_1424_chunk_128159).
The reversible Fe2+/3+ redox transitions optimize the binding strength of Fe centers with ORR intermediates, enhancing activity (doc_1424_chunk_128165).

PGM-free M–N–C single-atom catalysts (SACs) have insufficient intrinsic activity and density of active sites compared to PGM-based catalysts, limiting cathode layer thickness reduction and MEA performance. Fe-free M–N–C SACs like Sn–N–C have lower Fenton-reaction activity but inferior activity compared to Fe–N–C SACs. Designing dual single-atomic metal active sites can optimize adsorption strengths of key ORR intermediates. Increasing metal loadings is desired but aggregation during high-temperature pyrolysis is a challenge.
