PEMFCs operate with hydrogen oxidation reaction at the anode and oxygen reduction reaction at the cathode, with oxygen feed and proton conduction critical at the triple-phase boundary (doc_1424_chunk_128124).
Water management and liquid water accumulation at the anode can lead to ionomer swelling, performance loss, and cell drying affecting the cathode (doc_1424_chunk_128138).
High power densities in PEMFCs are achieved under wet humidity and low cathode loadings; challenges remain at higher loadings and translating RDE activity to MEA performance (doc_1424_chunk_128142).

The cell contains a Ni(OH)2/NiOOH mediator sandwiched by a CO2RR gas diffusion electrode (GDE) and a HOR GDE, to decouple CO2RR and HOR while eliminating sluggish oxygen catalysis. The cell operates in two alternating steps: Step 1 reduces CO2 to CO or formate at the CO2RR GDE while oxidizing Ni(OH)2 to NiOOH; Step 2 uses Ni electrode and HOR GDE for energy harvesting, reducing NiOOH back to Ni(OH)2.

The cell assembly includes a CO2RR GDE (1x1 cm2), a Ni(OH)2/NiOOH mediator supported on Ni foam (2x2 cm2), and a Pt/C GDE (1x1 cm2) for HOR, separated by a porous separator soaked in 1 M KOH. A Hg/HgO reference electrode is placed between the CO2RR GDE and the mediator.

Step 1: CO2 reduction at cathode to CO or formate with Ni(OH)2 oxidation to NiOOH at anode. Step 2: NiOOH reduction at cathode with hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) at anode regenerating Ni(OH)2. The reactions are temporally and spatially separated with alternating operation.

HOR catalyst (Pt/C) suffers poisoning by CO and other CO2RR products migrating through electrolyte or membrane, causing performance degradation. Ni(OH)2/NiOOH mediator prevents HOR catalyst poisoning by decoupling reactions and blocking product migration.

Both NiOR and NiRR of Ni(OH)2/NiOOH electrode, as well as the OER of Co3O4 on Ni foam (1 × 1 cm2) were studied in a three-electrode system. 1 M KOH solution was used as the electrolyte, a platinum plate (1 × 1 cm2) was used as the counter electrode, and a Hg/HgO electrode was used as the reference electrode. LSV and CV was performed at a scan rate of 1 mV s−1. The Tafel slope was calculated using the Tafel equation: η = b log(j) + a, where η, b, and j represent the overpotential, Tafel slope, and current density, respectively.

During the CO2RR test, CO2 was purged into the cathodic compartment, the measured outlet flow rate was ~27 mL min−1. EIS was recorded at frequencies ranging from 105 Hz to 0.01 Hz. ECSA was estimated by measuring an electric double layer capacitance from the scan-rate-dependent CV at various scan rates (20, 40, 60, 80, 100 and 120 mV s−1) in an Ar-saturated 0.5 M Na2SO4 electrolyte. The potential window was selected from −0.57 to −0.46 V vs. SCE. The capacitance (Cdl) was estimated by plotting the difference of charging current density versus the scan rate.

The cell can operate with membrane-free or membrane-based configurations. An anion-exchange membrane (AEM) was used in a modified cell to separate anode and cathode compartments, facilitating gas collection and maintaining electrolyte flow. The mediator prevents CO2 crossover related carbon loss and HOR catalyst poisoning.

CO2 is fed to the cathode side for electrochemical reduction to CO or formate. Hydrogen is fed to the anode side during Step 2 for hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) to regenerate Ni(OH)2 mediator and harvest energy.

The cell achieves high selectivity for CO (up to 81.9% FE at 150 mA cm−2) and formate (up to 95.3% FE at 150 mA cm−2) at voltages below 0.9 V (50 mA cm−2). Voltage efficiencies up to 78.4% at 20 mA cm−2 and stable operation over 100 h with minimal voltage degradation were demonstrated.

The H2-integrated CO2RR cell shows 0.87 V and 0.89 V cell voltages at 50 mA cm−2 for CO and formate generation, respectively, significantly lower than conventional CO2RR. Coupling with water electrolyzers (AWE or SOEC) reduces total energy consumption by up to 42%.

Hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) kinetics on Ru/C show higher exchange current density than Pt/C; Pt modification (3%Pt-Ru/C) further enhances intrinsic HOR activity with higher exchange current density; at high anodic potential (>0.1 V), Ru/C current density drops sharply due to deactivation, while 3%Pt-Ru/C maintains stable current density up to 0.8 V (doc_1567_chunk_141993, Results and discussion; doc_1567_chunk_141995, Results and discussion).

Number of electrons involved in HOR on Ru/C decreases from ~2 at low potential to ~0 at high potential, indicating hindered 2-electron HOR at high potential; 3%Pt-Ru/C retains ~2-electron process across potentials, showing Pt prevents Ru deactivation (doc_1567_chunk_141996, Results and discussion).

Deactivation of Ru during HOR is due to broken surface water network channel for H* oxidative desorption induced by Ru surface oxidation; Pt single atoms suppress Ru oxidation and preserve accessibility of interfacial water network, enabling stable HOR activity (doc_1567_chunk_141988, Results and discussion).

DFT and AIMD simulations show Pt-Ru(0001) surface has more electron-enriched surface than Ru(0001), weakening adsorption of O-containing species and preventing Ru surface oxidation; oxidation of Ru surface decreases interfacial water molecules and increases energy barrier for H* desorption, hindering HOR; Pt modification facilitates H* desorption by preserving interfacial water network (doc_1567_chunk_142012, Results and discussion; doc_1567_chunk_142016, Results and discussion).

Anion exchange membrane fuel cells (AEMFCs) were assembled using Ru/C and 3%Pt-Ru/C as anode catalysts; 3%Pt-Ru/C shows superior AEMFC activity and high durability with stable cell voltage output for at least 20 h, verifying feasibility of Pt-protection strategy in real device (doc_1567_chunk_141997, Results and discussion).

Electrochemical tests conducted in H2-saturated 0.1 M KOH electrolyte using rotating disk electrode (RDE) with 3-electrode system; polarization curves and exchange current densities obtained to evaluate HOR performance (doc_1567_chunk_141993, Results and discussion).

Operando XAS and SR-FTIR spectroscopy performed in H2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution to probe structural and interfacial water changes during HOR catalysis (doc_1567_chunk_141998, Results and discussion; doc_1567_chunk_142010, Results and discussion).

Ru/C exhibits higher intrinsic HOR activity than Pt/C at low potential but suffers rapid deactivation at high anodic potential; 3%Pt-Ru/C shows enhanced intrinsic activity and superior stability with maintained current density at high potential (up to 0.8 V) and less current decay over 400 min chronoamperometry test (doc_1567_chunk_141993, Results and discussion; doc_1567_chunk_141995, Results and discussion; doc_1567_chunk_141996, Results and discussion).

3%Pt-Ru/C catalyst assembled in AEMFC shows superior activity and stable cell voltage output for at least 20 h, demonstrating practical application potential (doc_1567_chunk_141997, Results and discussion).

Ru catalyst deactivation at high anodic potential (>0.1 V) is caused by severe surface oxidation leading to broken interfacial water network and hindered H* oxidative desorption, resulting in sharp current density drop; Pt single atoms prevent Ru oxidation and preserve interfacial water network, thus preventing deactivation (doc_1567_chunk_141995, Results and discussion; doc_1567_chunk_141998, Results and discussion; doc_1567_chunk_142010, Results and discussion).

The Ru-NPs/WCx catalyst enhances both hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) performances in alkaline conditions by optimizing the adsorption of intermediates such as *H and *OH due to strong electronic metal-support interaction (EMSI). The catalyst exhibits a high HOR mass activity of 6.5 A mgRu-1 at 50 mV, about 10-fold that of Pt/C, and a low HER overpotential of 16 mV at 10 mA cm-2, surpassing Pt/C and Ru/C.

The strong interaction between Ru nanoparticles and WCx support results in electron transfer from Ru to WCx, creating an electron-deficient state of Ru that weakens the Ru-H bond energy and strengthens OH adsorption, optimizing the adsorption of H_ad and OH_ad intermediates and boosting alkaline HOR/HER processes.

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations reveal that the Ru-NPs/WCx configuration exhibits the most significant HOR performance by simultaneously considering H binding energy (HBE) and OH binding energy (OHBE) mechanisms, indicating that both H and OH adsorption influence alkaline HOR/HER activities.

The electron withdrawing effect of WC_x support increases the oxidation state of Ru, weakening *H adsorption and enhancing *OH adsorption, which boosts the hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) performance via both HBE and OHBE pathways.

Anion exchange membrane water electrolyzers (AEMWE) were assembled using PiperION™ A80 (80 μm) membrane with Ru-NPs/WC_x or commercial Pt/C as cathode catalysts and NiFe-LDH as anode electrocatalyst, operating at 80 °C with 1.0 M KOH electrolyte.

The Ru-NPs/WCx catalyst was tested in hydrogen-air anion-exchange membrane fuel cells (AEMFCs) and alkaline anion-exchange membrane water electrolyzers (AEMWEs). The AEMFC with Ru-NPs/WCx anode (0.043 mgRu cm-2) and commercial Pt/C cathode delivers high peak power densities of 0.62 W cm-2 in H2-air (CO2-free) and 0.75 W cm-2 in H2-O2, with high current densities at 0.65 V. The AEMFC shows excellent durability over 210 h with only 0.9 mV h-1 voltage decay. The AEMWE with Ru-NPs/WCx cathode and NiFe-LDH anode requires only 1.73 V to reach 1 A cm-2 and maintains over 150 h at 500 mA cm-2 without obvious decay.
Electrochemical measurements were performed in H2-saturated 0.1 M KOH electrolyte (pH 13.0 ± 0.01) using a three-electrode system with rotation disk electrode (RDE) at 1600 rpm for HOR and HER tests.

The Ru-NPs/WCx catalyst exhibits high HOR activity with a current density of 2.46 mA cm-2 at 50 mV, surpassing Pt/C and Ru/C. It shows a high kinetic current density (jk) of 45.6 mA cm-2 at 50 mV, 7.0 and 10.6 times that of Pt/C and Ru/C respectively, and a geometric exchange current density (j0) of 3.36 mA cm-2. The mass activity (jk,m) is 6.5 A mgRu-1 at 50 mV, about 10 times that of Pt/C. The catalyst demonstrates excellent durability with negligible potential decay after 1000 cycles and retains ~80% current density after 20,000 s at 100 mV. It also shows superior CO tolerance compared to Pt/C and Ru/C.

In AEMFC tests, the Ru-NPs/WCx anode delivers a peak power density of 0.62 W cm-2 in H2-air and 0.75 W cm-2 in H2-O2, with mass activity of 3.13 A mgPGM-1 at 0.65 V and specific peak power density of 2.55 W mgPGM-1. The AEMFC maintains stable operation over 210 h with a voltage decay rate of 0.9 mV h-1. The catalyst also maintains performance under 100 ppm CO in H2 feed.

For HER, Ru-NPs/WCx shows an overpotential of 16 mV at 10 mA cm-2, outperforming Pt/C and Ru/C. It has a mass activity of 18.8 A mgRu-1 at 100 mV, 10.9 and 17.8 times higher than Pt/C and Ru/C respectively, and a Tafel slope of 31.1 mV dec-1 indicating fast reaction kinetics. The catalyst exhibits high turnover frequency (TOF) of 21.03 s-1 at 20 mV overpotential and nearly 100% Faradaic efficiency. The AEMWE with Ru-NPs/WCx cathode requires 1.73 V to reach 1 A cm-2 and shows durability over 150 h at 500 mA cm-2.

The copper organic polymers (MOPs) are one-dimensional Cu arrays with nanofiber structure due to strong π-π stacking between phenylene diacetylene units. The coordination structure involves σ-π dative bonding between Cu(I) ions and alkyne-based ligands, forming stable Cu^δ+ sites. The crystal structure shows poorly crystalline components with triclinic cell parameters. The Cu valence state in PhCu MOPs is approximately +1, confirmed by XPS and XANES, with no metallic copper or Cu^2+ detected. EXAFS spectra reveal Cu-C bonds and Cu-Cu interactions consistent with DFT simulations. (chunk_id: doc_1616_chunk_147555, doc_1616_chunk_147560, doc_1616_chunk_147562, doc_1616_chunk_147563, doc_1616_ch

The CO2 reduction reaction (CO2RR) proceeds via adsorption of *CO intermediates on Cu^δ+ sites. Methoxyl functionalization reduces the energy barrier for the potential-determining step (CO2 to *COOH) by 0.23 eV, enhancing reactivity. Strong *CO adsorption favors methane production, while weakened *CO adsorption (e.g., with fluorine substitution) leads to desorption and less reduction. The energy barrier for C-C coupling on OMe-PhCu is higher than for hydrogenation of single *CO, favoring C1 methane pathway. In situ Raman and ATR-IR spectra confirm atop-adsorbed *CO intermediates on OMe-PhCu. (chunk_id: doc_1616_chunk_147581, doc_1616_chunk_147582, doc_1616_chunk_147583)

A proton exchange membrane (PEM) electrolyzer was constructed using the OMe-PhCu catalyst as cathode and hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) as the anode reaction instead of oxygen evolution reaction (OER). Nafion 211 membrane was pretreated and used as the PEM. The PEM system demonstrated lower cell voltage and good stability at 200 mA cm^-2, maintaining considerable methane Faradaic efficiency. (chunk_id: doc_1616_chunk_147572, doc_1616_chunk_147607)

The CO2 electroreduction was performed in acidic electrolyte (1.0 M K2SO4, pH=3±0.05) with CO2 flow rate controlled at 35 sccm. The single-pass carbon efficiency (SPCE) increased to 24.1% at 3 mL min^-1 CO2 flow rate at 500 mA cm^-2 current density. The electrolyte composition and pH were carefully controlled and monitored. (chunk_id: doc_1616_chunk_147572, doc_1616_chunk_147606)

The OMe-PhCu catalyst exhibits high methane Faradaic efficiency (FE) of 68.8% at 300 mA cm^-2 in acidic electrolyte, with a partial current density of 324.5 mA cm^-2. The FE decreases slightly at higher current densities but remains competitive. The catalyst shows broad pH versatility with good performance also in alkaline electrolyte. The PEM electrolyzer shows good stability at 200 mA cm^-2. The catalyst maintains stable Cu^δ+ oxidation state and nanofiber morphology after prolonged CO2RR operation. (chunk_id: doc_1616_chunk_147569, doc_1616_chunk_147571, doc_1616_chunk_147573, doc_1616_chunk_147574, doc_1616_chunk_147577)

Common copper compounds such as Cu2-xS and copper phthalocyanine undergo structural reconstruction during CO2RR in acidic conditions, transforming into metallic copper or copper clusters. Cu2-xS nanocages reconstruct into cracked nanocrystals, and CuPc evolves into stacked nanosheets after CO2RR. In contrast, OMe-PhCu retains its initial nanofiber morphology with negligible changes, demonstrating resistance to reconstruction. Degradation in PEM electrolyzer performance is partly attributed to water flooding, salt precipitation, and carbon deposition on catalyst surface. (chunk_id: doc_1616_chunk_147551, doc_1616_chunk_147575, doc_1616_chunk_147573)

Electrochemical hydrogenation performed with concentrated HMF feedstocks ranging from 20 mM to 1000 mM in 0.5 M PBS electrolyte; Ami-Cu3N/CF maintains high DHMF yield and Faradaic efficiency (~99%) and high productivity (63.4 mmol cm-2 h-1) at 1000 mM HMF (chunk_id: doc_1621_chunk_148090, section_title: Results).

Ami-Cu3N/CF achieves >99% DHMF selectivity and >98% Faradaic efficiency with production rate of 63.4 mmol cm-2 h-1 at 500 mA cm-2 under concentrated HMF conditions; exhibits higher intrinsic turnover frequency (17.2 s-1 at -0.35 VRHE) than Cu3N/CF and Cu/CF (chunk_id: doc_1621_chunk_148069, section_title: Abstract),(chunk_id: doc_1621_chunk_148089, section_title: Results).

Ami-Cu3N/CF shows stable potential and high selectivity (>98% FE, >99% DHMF selectivity) across current densities from -100 to -600 mA cm-2 and maintains performance over 90 cycles, outperforming Cu3N/CF and Cu/CF (chunk_id: doc_1621_chunk_148086, section_title: Results),(chunk_id: doc_1621_chunk_148090, section_title: Results).

Paired electrolysis system coupling cathodic HMF hydrogenation with anodic HMF oxidation reduces cell voltage and improves energy efficiency compared to conventional OER-coupled HMF reduction (chunk_id: doc_1621_chunk_148092, section_title: Results).

The Ami-Cu3N/CF catalyst achieves high Faradaic efficiency (FE) and electrochemical hydrogenation (ECH) activity under elevated HMF concentrations, with FE exceeding 98% at an operating potential of -26 mV and FE for DHMF above 90% even at 1000 mM HMF concentration. The dual-electrode flow electrolytic cell lowers cell voltage and energy consumption, improving economic feasibility of biomass upgrading and demonstrating significant practical potential.

The Ni complexes can exist in three different conformers — endo–endo, endo–exo and exo–exo — that differ in the position of the pendant amines or ammoniums relative to Ni, affecting catalytic activity.

H2 reversibly binds CyArg at temperatures at which H2 oxidation becomes reversible, and interactions between side chains alter the distance between the pendant amine and the metal, enhancing H2 addition.
Coupling of H+ and e− transfers can have a considerable impact on the accessibility of catalytic intermediates, lowering overpotential requirements.
The catalytic activity depends on solvent, with H2O favoring the more active endo–endo conformer over endo–exo and exo–exo conformers, affecting H2 coordination rates.
The turnover frequency (TOF) and overpotential (η) are key performance metrics; high TOF and low η are desired for energy-efficient catalysis.
Ni complexes with amino acid-derived ligands exhibit electrochemical reversibility for H2 oxidation and production with overpotentials approaching those of hydrogenase enzymes (~150 mV).
There is a strong correlation between TOF and overpotential for Ni complexes; catalysts operating near equilibrium potential have lower TOF, while those at higher overpotential are faster.
Inclusion of pendant amines lowers overpotential by ~600 mV compared to analogous complexes without pendant amines.
Hydrogenases are unstable outside narrow conditions (pH 4–8, 20–40 °C, absence of O2), limiting practical use.
Molecular Ni catalysts show notable instability in the presence of O2, and surface-confined catalysts cannot be replenished once deactivated.
The electrochemical cell uses Fe cathode and Zn sacrificial anode in a divided cell, with Cu(OTf)2 and KOtBu additives, and nBu4NI/NMP electrolyte saturated with CO2 under constant current.
Undivided cell conditions use Pt anode and substoichiometric KOtBu, with nBu4NI electrolyte, enabling paired electrolysis and continuous generation of I2 as hydrogen-atom acceptor.

Undivided cell configuration allows both anodic and cathodic reactions in same chamber, enabling paired electrolysis and continuous regeneration of iodine species, which is critical for C4 carboxylation selectivity.

The r-Pb electrode undergoes structural transformation from Pb–PbSO4 to Pb–PbCO3 during CO2 electrolysis, forming a Pb–PbCO3 composite that is thermodynamically stable in the PEM system (doc_1662_chunk_151737, section_title='').

CO2 reduction reaction (CO2RR) in a strong acid operated under a proton-exchange membrane (PEM) system converts CO2 to formic acid at the cathode with hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) at the anode (doc_1662_chunk_151728, section_title='').

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations show that the Pb–PbCO3 interface exhibits the lowest free energy upshift for CO2RR towards formic acid, favoring CO2 adsorption and hydrogenation steps over hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), ensuring high activity and selectivity in acidic electrolytes (doc_1662_chunk_151744, section_title='').

The proton-exchange membrane (PEM) system uses a Nafion 212 membrane and is durable, enabling continuous operation for more than 5,200 hours with stable performance (doc_1662_chunk_151733, section_title='').

Utilization of hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) at the anode instead of water oxidation reaction (WOR) decreases cell voltage and avoids generation of harmful hydrogen peroxide that could degrade and destroy the PEM, contributing to membrane durability and long system life (doc_1662_chunk_151734, section_title='').

The cathode feed is high-purity CO2 gas at controlled flow rates (optimized at 3 sccm for high single-pass conversion efficiency), and the anode feed is high-purity humid hydrogen gas at 20 sccm for HOR (doc_1662_chunk_151774, section_title='').

The PEM system achieves formic acid production with over 93% Faradaic efficiency, a current density of 1.2 A cm^-2 at 2.4 V cell voltage, and nearly 91% single-pass CO2 conversion efficiency at 600 mA cm^-2 and 2.2 V (doc_1662_chunk_151730, section_title='').

The system operates continuously for more than 5,200 hours with stable performance and excellent pH tolerance, maintaining high Faradaic efficiency over a wide pH range except at pH 0 due to excessive HER kinetics (doc_1662_chunk_151733, section_title='').

A scaled-up PEM reactor with 5×5 cm^2 electrode area achieves 15 A current at roughly 2.7 V with over 91% Faradaic efficiency for formic acid generation, demonstrating scalability (doc_1662_chunk_151736, section_title='').

Electrolyte overflow induced by electrowetting of the three-phase interface can cause system failure; stability is maintained by reprocessing the gas diffusion electrode with PTFE emulsion every 200 hours or by using PTFE membrane-supported r-Pb catalysts (doc_1662_chunk_151734, section_title='').
Electrodeposited platinum onto SSCs to create active hydrogen oxidation electrodes (Pt/SSC).
Hydrogen oxidation at anode produces protons of controlled thermodynamic activity, avoiding solvent oxidation and enabling continuous ammonia production.

Coupling hydrogen oxidation at Pt/SSC anode with lithium-mediated nitrogen reduction at SSC cathode enables electrochemical Haber–Bosch reactor producing ammonia from nitrogen and hydrogen at ambient conditions.
Proton donor ethanol consumed at cathode to produce ammonia and ethoxide; ethanol regenerated at anode from ethoxide via hydrogen oxidation, enabling proton donor recycling.
Ammonia partial current density of 8.8 ± 1.4 mA cm−2 and Faradaic efficiency of 35 ± 6% achieved using lithium-mediated approach with SSC cathode.
Hydrogen oxidation current densities up to 25 mA cm−2 with near-unity Faradaic efficiency achieved in two non-aqueous solvents using Pt/SSC anodes.
Electrochemical Haber–Bosch reactor produces ammonia at ambient conditions with Faradaic efficiency up to 47.5 ± 4%.
Coupling electrochemical Haber–Bosch reactor with water electrolyzer produces ammonia from nitrogen, water, and renewable electrons with Faradaic efficiency of 30 ± 2%.

The redox-active film consists of [FeFe] hydrogenase embedded in a low-potential, 2,2´-viologen modified hydrogel polymer (PV2) with a polyvinyl-alcohol backbone, designed to match the redox potential of the 2H+/H2 couple at pH 7. The polymer has a high viologen load (~48% w/w) and the viologen moiety is covalently grafted via a diisocyanate linker. The polymer film mediates electron transfer between the enzyme and the electrode.

The polymer film with embedded hydrogenase catalyses reversible hydrogen oxidation and evolution with high catalytic currents (mA cm-2 range) under mediated electron transfer (MET) conditions. The catalytic waves are centered on the pH-independent redox potential of the viologen mediator in the film, and the catalytic bias changes with pH. The catalytic current scales with the amount of enzyme/polymer deposited in thin films.
The hydrogenase enzyme used is the [FeFe] hydrogenase from Desulfovibrio desulfuricans (DdHydAB).

The polymer film mediates electron transfer between the electrode and the hydrogenase enzyme via the viologen redox moieties, enabling bidirectional catalysis of hydrogen oxidation and evolution. Electron transfer is slower than catalysis, but reversibility is maintained due to the matching redox potentials of the mediator and enzyme.

A kinetic model describes the mediated bidirectional electrocatalysis in the film, relating the catalytic current to bimolecular electron transfer rate constants between mediator and enzyme, catalytic rate constants for hydrogen oxidation and evolution, and the redox potentials of the mediator and enzyme. The catalytic potential is close to the mediator potential, indicating slower intermolecular electron transfer than catalysis.

The embedded [FeFe] hydrogenase catalyses reversible hydrogen oxidation and evolution reactions with high rates and low overpotential in the polymer film. The catalytic bias (ratio of oxidation to reduction current) changes with pH, favoring hydrogen production at lower pH.

The reversible catalysis enables the polymer/hydrogenase electrode to function efficiently as an anode for hydrogen oxidation in H2/O2 biofuel cells and as a cathode for hydrogen evolution in electrolysers, with high Faraday efficiency and low overpotential.

The biofuel cells operate with hydrogen gas (H2) at the anode and oxygen gas (O2) at the cathode, with the hydrogenase/polymer film catalyzing H2 oxidation and bilirubin oxidase catalyzing O2 reduction.
The electrolyser cathode operates in aqueous buffer (Tris-citrate, pH 6.4) with KCl supporting electrolyte, producing hydrogen gas at low overpotential and high Faraday efficiency.

The hydrogenase/polymer cathode in electrolysers produces hydrogen with Faraday efficiencies close to 100% and energy conversion efficiencies above 95% at current densities up to 4 mA cm-2 and overpotentials as low as 12 mV.

The catalyst consists of primary fibrous carbon particles and secondary few-layer graphene sheets, with carbon phases dominated by small, randomly oriented graphitic domains forming the predominant carbon phase. The surfaces of fibrous carbon particles are dominated by exposed edges and steps of graphite (002) basal planes, contributing to high ORR activity. The catalyst has a hierarchical pore structure with mesopores and micropores, with a BET surface area of ~1500 m2/g and micropore surface area ~1600 m2/g, which is a primary factor governing ORR activity.

The oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) at the cathode is inherently slower than the hydrogen oxidation reaction at the anode and is catalyzed by carbon-embedded nitrogen-coordinated iron (FeN4) active sites. Quantum chemical modeling shows that edge-hosted FeN4 sites have higher ORR activity than bulk-hosted FeN4 sites, with edge-hosted sites binding O2 and having a limiting potential of 0.80 V, indicating high ORR activity.

The cathode catalyst layers (CCLs) were fabricated with Nafion ionomer content varying from 35 to 60 wt %, where increased ionomer content improved current densities at fuel cell voltages >0.7 V by more effective filling and lining of macropores, improving catalyst utilization, but hindered water removal at high ionomer content caused performance decrease in the mass transport region.

Fuel cell performance was evaluated under realistic hydrogen-air conditions at 1.0 bar partial pressure of H2 and air, with air flow rates varied from 200 to 760 standard cubic centimeters per minute. Higher air flow improved current densities and maximum power density, indicating mass transport limitations in the cathode catalyst layer.

Experiments under H2-O2 conditions at partial pressures of 0.3, 1.0, and 2.0 bar O2 were performed to minimize mass transport effects and better assess intrinsic catalyst activity, achieving maximum power densities of ~0.87 and 0.94 W/cm2 at 1.0 and 2.0 bar O2, respectively, the highest reported for PGM-free ORR catalysts operating on oxygen.

The (CM+PANI)-Fe-C catalyst achieved current densities in the kinetic region (>0.75 V) comparable to a Pt cathode at 0.1 mgPt/cm2 loading under hydrogen-air conditions, with a maximum power density of 0.39 W/cm2 at 1.0 bar partial pressure of H2 and air. Increasing ionomer content improved current densities at voltages >0.7 V but reduced performance in the mass transport region due to hindered water removal.

Rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE) testing showed a half-wave potential of 0.80 V vs RHE with a low H2O2 yield (<2.5%) and an average electron transfer number >3.95, ranking among the highest ORR activity and selectivity for PGM-free catalysts reported. Catalyst performance loss after 30,000 RDE cycles was ~5%.
The hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) in alkaline medium involves the oxidative desorption of hydrogen from Pd as the rate determining step: Pd-H + OH- → Pd + H2O + e-.
The presence of ceria in contact with Pd weakens the Pd-H bonding, enhancing HOR kinetics and increasing stability under alkaline conditions.
The Pd/C/CeO2 catalyst has significantly higher activity than Pd/C towards the hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) in alkaline medium.
Hydrogen pump experiments used hydrogen feed at 100 sccm flow rate and 60 °C with 100% relative humidity to evaluate HOR catalysts.
The 10 wt% Pd loading on C-CeO2 catalyst shows optimized HOR activity with highest mass activity and smallest Pd crystallite size compared to 6 wt% and 20 wt% Pd samples.
Hydrogen pumping tests show Pd/C-CeO2 (10 wt%) catalyst outperforms Pd/C (10 wt%) and approaches Pt/C performance with ~10 times lower Pd loading.

Fuel cell tests confirm the 10 wt% Pd/C-CeO2 catalyst has slightly better performance than 6 wt% and 20 wt% Pd loadings, with higher Pd loadings leading to Pd deposition on carbon regions that do not enhance activity.
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy shows Pd/C-CeO2 catalyst has lower charge transfer resistance and similar high-frequency resistance to Pt/C, indicating improved HOR kinetics compared to Pd/C.
The Pd/C/CeO2 bifunctional material studied is the HOR electrocatalyst with highest activity and stability for alkaline exchange membrane fuel cells (AEM-FCs) so far developed.
Catalyst stability under harsh potential cycling is improved for Pd/C/CeO2 compared to Pd/C.

The structure of alkaline HOR electrocatalysts is sensitive to size, morphology, composition and support, which are pivotal for tuning alkaline HOR activity of PGM-based electrocatalysts (chunk_id=doc_1723_chunk_157225, section_title=5.1 PGM-based alkaline HOR electrocatalysts).

examples.section_title: 5.1.1 Size-dependent alkaline HOR activity of PGM-based electrocatalysts
examples.value: Size-dependent alkaline HOR activity of Pt/C shows maximum mass activity at approximately 3 nm particle size, with specific activity increasing with size and leveling off; Ru/C shows volcano-shaped mass and specific activity with size, peaking at 3 nm; Ir/C and Pd/C show increasing specific activity with size, with mass activity decreasing with size (chunk_id=doc_1723_chunk_157226 to doc_1723_chunk_157230, section_title=5.1.1 Size-dependent alkaline HOR activity of PGM-based electrocatalysts).

examples.section_title: 5.1.1 Size-dependent alkaline HOR activity of PGM-based electrocatalysts
examples.value: Morphology affects alkaline HOR activity: Pt nanowires show better performance than commercial Pt/C due to crystalline planes and fewer defects; Ru@Pt core-shell electrocatalysts show enhanced activity depending on morphology and composition (chunk_id=doc_1723_chunk_157231, section_title=5.1.1 Size-dependent alkaline HOR activity of PGM-based electrocatalysts).

examples.section_title: 5.1.3 Composition-dependent alkaline HOR activity of PGM-based electrocatalysts
examples.value: Composition influences alkaline HOR activity: Pt7M3 (M=Ru, Fe, Co) alloy nanowires show higher activity than Pt NWs due to electronic interactions; Pt7Cu3 and Pt7Au3 NWs show lower activity due to surface oxide formation or increased HBE; Ni-Ag alloys with specific compositions have hydrogen adsorption sites favorable for alkaline HOR (chunk_id=doc_1723_chunk_157232 to doc_1723_chunk_157233, section_title=5.1.3 Composition-dependent alkaline HOR activity of PGM-based electrocatalysts).

examples.section_title: 3. Mechanism of the HOR in alkaline media
examples.value: The alkaline HOR overall reaction is H2 + 2 OH- -> 2 H2O + 2 e- (chunk_id=doc_1723_chunk_157194, section_title=3. Mechanism of the HOR in alkaline media).

examples.section_title: 3. Mechanism of the HOR in alkaline media
examples.value: Alkaline HOR proceeds via Tafel-Volmer or Heyrovsky-Volmer mechanisms with elementary steps: Tafel (H2 + 2M -> 2 M-Had), Heyrovsky (H2 + OH- + M -> M-Had + H2O + e-), and Volmer (Had + OH- -> H2O + e- + M) (chunk_id=doc_1723_chunk_157195 to doc_1723_chunk_157198, section_title=3. Mechanism of the HOR in alkaline media).

examples.section_title: 3.1 HOR pathway with OH $^{-}$ in the solution
examples.value: The rate-determining step (RDS) in alkaline HOR is debated: Tafel step as RDS supported by reaction rate and Tafel slope (~30 mV/dec); Heyrovsky step as RDS supported by kinetic expressions and DFT; Volmer step as RDS supported by charge transfer resistance, Butler-Volmer fitting, and activation free energy calculations (chunk_id=doc_1723_chunk_157202 to doc_1723_chunk_157209, section_title=3.1 HOR pathway with OH- in the solution).

examples.section_title: 3.2 HOR pathway with $OH_{ad}$ on an electrocatalyst surface
examples.value: An alternative alkaline HOR pathway involves adsorbed hydroxyl species (OHad) on the electrocatalyst surface, which may facilitate removal of Had and enhance activity; oxophilic metals like Ir, Ru, Cu, Ni incorporated into catalysts improve alkaline HOR activity via this mechanism (chunk_id=doc_1723_chunk_157210 to doc_1723_chunk_157213, section_title=3.2 HOR pathway with OHad on an electrocatalyst surface).

examples.section_title: 5.1.3 Composition-dependent alkaline HOR activity of PGM-based electrocatalysts
examples.value: The alkaline HOR activity of Ru_xPt_y/C is enhanced due to incorporation of Ru into Pt, reducing activation energy of Heyrovsky/Volmer step and changing RDS to Tafel step.

examples.section_title: 5.1.3 Composition-dependent alkaline HOR activity of PGM-based electrocatalysts
examples.value: Alkaline HOR on Ru_xPd_y/C proceeds through Heyrovsky/Volmer step as RDS due to insufficient interaction between Ru and Pd to reduce activation energy.

Electrocatalytic hydrogenation (ECH) of 5-(hydroxymethyl)furfural (HMF) to 2,5-bis(hydroxymethyl)furan (BHMF) on OD-Ag electrode with high selectivity up to 98.2% and enhanced activity compared to Ag foil (chunk_id: doc_1727_chunk_157649, section_title: )

Pairing ECH of HMF to BHMF with anodic oxidation of HMF to 2,5-furandicarboxylic acid (FDCA) mediated by TEMPO reduces cell voltage from ~7.5 V to ~2.0 V in flow cell, increasing energy efficiency more than four-fold at 10 mA (chunk_id: doc_1727_chunk_157649, doc_1727_chunk_157692, doc_1727_chunk_157693, doc_1727_chunk_157695, section_title: )

Anodic hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) on Pt further reduces cell voltage to ~0.85 V at 10 mA when paired with ECH of HMF on OD-Ag (chunk_id: doc_1727_chunk_157649, doc_1727_chunk_157703, doc_1727_chunk_157704, section_title: )

Catholyte containing 20 mM HMF in 0.5 M borate buffer solution (pH 9.2) used for ECH; anolyte contains TEMPO mediator for ECO of HMF to FDCA or hydrogen gas for HOR (chunk_id: doc_1727_chunk_157676, doc_1727_chunk_157694, doc_1727_chunk_157703, section_title: Results and discussion)

OD-Ag electrode shows 240 mV positive shift in onset potential and 2–3 times higher current density than Ag foil for HMF reduction; BHMF selectivity above 90% over broad potential range (-1.05 to -1.35 V vs Ag/AgCl) with long-term stability (chunk_id: doc_1727_chunk_157668, doc_1727_chunk_157678, section_title: Results and discussion)

Flow cell configuration enhances mass transport and reduces ohmic loss, resulting in higher HMF conversion and BHMF selectivity compared to H-type cell; cell voltage reduced from ~7.5 V to ~2.0 V at 10 mA for ECH–ECO paired electrolysis with combined faradaic efficiency of 163% (chunk_id: doc_1727_chunk_157677, doc_1727_chunk_157695, doc_1727_chunk_157700, section_title: Results and discussion)

ECH–HOR paired electrolysis achieves cell voltage of only ~0.85 V at 10 mA with BHMF selectivity of 82.3% (chunk_id: doc_1727_chunk_157703, doc_1727_chunk_157704, section_title: Results and discussion)

The redox-relay electrode (RR-electrode) contains AB-type hydrogen storage alloys, specifically LaNi5-type alloys, with a hexagonal CaCu5-type structure (space group P6/mmm). The RR-electrode is prepared by pasting carbon cloth with an ink containing alloy powder (20–100 μm), conducting carbon black, and PTFE binder (90:5:5 wt%), then vacuum dried. The alloy particles are mostly below 100 μm in size. (chunk_id: doc_1730_chunk_157975, section_title: Results and discussion)

Divided cells using membranes alleviate the stringent requirements for paired electrolysis but introduce cost, stability, and substrate crossover issues, complicating practical applications. (chunk_id: doc_1730_chunk_157966, section_title: Introduction)
The RR-electrode shows stable discharge capacity for 50 cycles after activation, indicating good cycling stability. (chunk_id: doc_1730_chunk_157980, section_title: Results and discussion)

The paired electrosynthesis strategy achieves high yields (e.g., 93% benzoic acid and 95% hydrogenation product) with 67% hydrogen atom utilization efficiency at optimal current density of 5 mA/cm2. (chunk_id: doc_1730_chunk_157997, section_title: Results and discussion)

The automated robotic platform enables continuous multigram-scale paired electrosynthesis with stable RR-electrode performance over 530 hours, producing grams of benzoic acid and 3-phenylpropanamide with yields above 90%. (chunk_id: doc_1730_chunk_158008, section_title: Results and discussion)

Attempts to pair electrooxidation of alcohols and hydrogenation of unsaturated compounds via conventional paired electrolysis strategies, including undivided cells and divided cells with cation/anion exchange membranes, failed to give acceptable yields after extensive condition screening. (chunk_id: doc_1730_chunk_157998, section_title: Results and discussion)

Ni2P electrocatalyst shows potential-dependent phase evolution during furfural electrooxidation: at 1.30-1.40 V (vs. RHE), Ni2P mediates oxidation to furoic acid (FAC) via surface-adsorbed hydroxyl groups while maintaining stability; at 1.40-1.60 V, dynamic surface reconstruction forms Ni2P-Ni(OH)2 heterostructure enhancing active sites and charge transfer, increasing conversion efficiency to 85% with 87% faradaic efficiency; beyond 1.60 V, oxygen evolution reaction dominates causing surface passivation.

Re-doped hydroxides enhance bifunctional activity toward both furfural electrooxidation (ECO) and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) by modulating electronic structures and oxygen vacancy concentrations, optimizing electron transfer and intermediate adsorption, lowering reaction energy barriers, and boosting selectivity and conversion efficiency in furfural to furoic acid transformations.

Zheng et al. encapsulated alcohol dehydrogenase (ADH) within a conjugated covalent organic framework (TpBD COF) to construct a composite bioelectrode (TpBD@ADH), where the conjugated framework enables efficient electron transfer and the hollow architecture reduces substrate diffusion resistance and protects enzymatic activity, integrating structural and electronic optimization to enhance catalytic efficiency and specificity.

Chamoulaud et al. fabricated copper-modified and nickel-modified porous graphite felts as cathode and anode respectively, enhancing electrocatalytic activity and selectivity toward FA and FAC production in divergent paired electrolysis of FF.

Zhang et al. synthesized Cu_3P and Ni_2P phases onto carbon fiber cloth, optimizing *H intermediate generation and utilization mechanisms at the electrode–electrolyte interface to elevate product selectivity and Faradaic efficiency.

Bharath et al. developed a Ru/RGO nanocomposite with a highly porous architecture, integrating Ru catalytic activity and RGO conductivity to enhance catalytic performance and achieve high FF conversion and carbon balance.

Ketkaew et al. synthesized Pt/Au Janus particles with spatially segregated Pt and Au regions, enabling simultaneous ECH and ECO of FF by generating a potential gradient across the particle surfaces, providing a novel strategy for FF divergent paired electrolysis.

Paired electrolysis mechanisms include parallel paired electrolysis with independent oxidation and reduction reactions producing distinct products; divergent paired electrolysis with same reactant undergoing different conversions at cathode and anode producing two distinct products; linear paired electrolysis with synergistic oxidation and reduction of single reactant forming unified product; and convergent paired electrolysis with coupling of reactive intermediates from both electrodes forming single product.

In parallel paired electrolysis, anodic electrooxidation of furfural can be coupled with hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) at cathode to enhance hydrogen production efficiency and reduce energy consumption by replacing oxygen evolution reaction (OER) with furfural oxidation.

Phosphorus in Cu-N2-P2 catalyst acts as the primary electron donor, optimizing charge distribution at Cu sites, reducing energy barrier for *CHO to *CH3O hydrogenation, enhancing intermediate adsorption and C-O bond cleavage, achieving methane FE of 73% at -1.6 V vs RHE.

Membranes play a critical role in reactor design, influencing water availability, ion transport, and reaction environment. Common membranes include diaphragm separators, PEMs, AEMs, BPMs, and ISMs. Membranes tailored for neutral pH face challenges such as reduced ionic conductivity and contamination risks. Surface-modified membranes and functionalized nanomaterials improve ion selectivity, resist fouling, and prevent degradation, enhancing stability and selectivity in paired electrolysis systems (doc_1757_chunk_160523, doc_1757_chunk_160529, doc_1757_chunk_160530).

Paired electrolysis involves anodic and cathodic reactions where anodic reactions can valorize waste or low-value feedstocks (e.g., plastics, biomass derivatives) into higher-value products, while cathodic reactions commonly include hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), CO2 reduction, and nitrogen reduction. Reaction pairing is critical to ensure charge balance, optimize efficiency, and maximize electron economy (doc_1757_chunk_160510, doc_1757_chunk_160533, doc_1757_chunk_160534).

Feedstocks for anodic reactions include chloride ions from seawater, biomass derivatives like HMF, glycerol, ethylene glycol, and 1,2-propanediol. Cathodic reactants include water, CO2, and nitrogen species. The availability and cost of these feedstocks influence the economic viability of paired electrolysis (doc_1757_chunk_160534, doc_1757_chunk_160535, doc_1757_chunk_160536).

Paired electrolysis performance is evaluated by key performance indicators (KPIs) such as current density, Faradaic efficiency (FE), selectivity, energy efficiency, and long-term stability. Industrial targets include current densities ≥1 A/cm2 and FE between 60% and 96% at 0.2–2.0 A/cm2. Catalyst design and reactor engineering are essential to meet these KPIs for various anodic reactions including biomass valorization, organic electrosynthesis, commodity chemical upgrading, and plastic upcycling (doc_1757_chunk_160567, doc_1757_chunk_160568, doc_1757_chunk_160569, doc_1757_chunk_160570, doc_1757_chunk_160571, doc_1757_chunk_160572).

Paired electrolysis faces failure modes including membrane fouling and degradation due to organic electrolytes or redox-active species, product and anion crossover reducing purity and yield, and surface site competition at electrodes reducing efficiency. Strategies to mitigate these include selective catalysts, surface-modified membranes, antifouling agents, and catalytic nanoparticles (doc_1757_chunk_160559, doc_1757_chunk_160560, doc_1757_chunk_160561, doc_1757_chunk_160529, doc_1757_chunk_160530).

Anodic reactions involve direct and indirect oxidation pathways, with indirect oxidation mediated by inorganic intermediates like hydroxyl radicals facilitating organic compound oxidation. Most small molecules have standard oxidation potentials lower than OER, but slow kinetics require higher anodic potentials. Multi-electron reactions such as HMF to FDCA oxidation involve complex mechanisms and rate-determining steps. High current densities (e.g., 1 A/cm2) can lead to anodic potentials up to 3–5 V vs RHE, with total cell voltages reaching 6–8 V. Catalyst optimization and electrolyte/membrane conductivity are critical to reduce overpotentials and ohmic losses (doc_1757_chunk_160542, doc_1757

Membranes critically influence ion transport, water availability, and reaction environment in paired electrolysis. Common types include diaphragm separators, proton exchange membranes (PEMs), anion exchange membranes (AEMs), bipolar membranes (BPMs), and ion-solvating membranes (ISMs). Membranes designed for neutral pH face challenges such as reduced ionic conductivity and contamination risks. Surface modifications with materials like graphene oxide and functionalized nanomaterials enhance ion selectivity, resist fouling, and prevent degradation, improving stability and selectivity (doc_1757_chunk_160523, doc_1757_chunk_160529, doc_1757_chunk_160530).

Hydrogen permeable palladium membranes enable full electron economy by selectively allowing hydrogen atoms to pass, facilitating simultaneous anodic and cathodic valorization with reduced cross-contamination. This approach enhances efficiency and flexibility in paired electrolysis systems (doc_1757_chunk_160588).

Anodic feedstocks include plastics (e.g., PET, PBT, PE, PVC, PS), biomass derivatives (e.g., hydroxymethyl-furfural, glycerol, ethylene glycol, 1,2-propanediol), chloride ions from seawater, and other low-cost feedstocks. Cathodic feedstocks commonly include water, CO2, and nitrogen species. The availability and cost of these feedstocks influence the economic viability and scalability of paired electrolysis (doc_1757_chunk_160510, doc_1757_chunk_160534, doc_1757_chunk_160535, doc_1757_chunk_160536).

Key performance indicators (KPIs) for paired electrolysis include current density, Faradaic efficiency (FE), selectivity, energy efficiency, and long-term stability. Industrial targets are current densities ≥1 A/cm2 and FE between 60% and 96% at 0.2–2.0 A/cm2. Catalyst and reactor design improvements have enabled selective electrooxidation of biomass and organic molecules at industrially relevant current densities with high FE and selectivity (doc_1757_chunk_160567, doc_1757_chunk_160568, doc_1757_chunk_160569, doc_1757_chunk_160570, doc_1757_chunk_160571, doc_1757_chunk_160572, doc_1757_chunk_160573).

Ni N/Ni interfacial electrocatalysts were synthesized through cathodic electrodeposition of porous Ni microspheres on current collectors such as Ni foam (NF) or carbon foam (CF), followed by thermal nitridation in ammonia to create rich Ni3N/Ni interfacial sites. The nitridation temperature was optimized to 300 °C for 6 h to obtain the best HER activity. The Ni3N/Ni/NF possessed a 3D macroporous ligament network structure with numerous stacked coarse particles over the skeleton surface, with uniform distribution of Ni and N elements. XRD patterns showed hexagonal Ni3N and cubic Ni phases. HRTEM images revealed clear interfaces between hexagonal Ni3N and cubic Ni with well-resolved lattice fr
The Ni3N/Ni/NF synthesized at 300 °C for 6 h with 41.82 wt.% Ni3N exhibited the highest HER activity, while longer nitridation duration decreased HER activity.

Ni3N/Ni/NF exhibits extraordinary HER performance with nearly zero onset overpotential and requires only 12 to 19 mV overpotential to produce −10 mA cm−2 current density in neutral and alkaline electrolytes. It also shows superior HOR activity in alkaline medium (0.1 M KOH) with great CO tolerance. The exchange current density for HOR on Ni3N/Ni/NF is 3.08 mA cm−2, 1.4 times that of Pt/NF and 17.6 times that of Ni/NF.

DFT calculations show that the interfacial sites between Ni3N and Ni have hydrogen adsorption free energy (ΔG_H*) values close to zero (0.01 and -0.07 eV), optimal for HER/HOR. The interface also favors water adsorption and reduces the activation energy barrier for water dissociation (0.50 eV), lower than on pure Ni or Ni3N, facilitating HER and HOR in alkaline and neutral electrolytes.

The Ni3N/Ni interface shows strong H2 adsorption and ideal ΔG_H* value, supporting excellent HOR performance. Experimental results confirm Ni3N/Ni/NF has higher specific activity than Pt/NF for HOR and superior CO tolerance, with only 3.5% decrease in HOR current density in 2% CO/H2 mixture compared to 63% decrease for Pt/NF.

In a small potential window of the micro-polarization region near the equilibrium potential (±20 mV vs. RHE), the Butler–Volmer equation can be expanded by Taylor’s formula and simplified as i = i_0 * (η F)/(R T). The exchange current density (j) was calculated by dividing i_0 by the geometric electrode area.

The electrocatalytic performance was tested in aqueous electrolytes: 1.0 M potassium phosphate buffer (pH 7.17) and 1.0 M KOH (pH 13.80) for HER, and 0.1 M KOH (pH 12.80) for HOR. Electrolytes were saturated with H2 during experiments. CO tolerance was tested using H2 gas mixture containing 2% CO (v/v).

Ni3N/Ni/NF shows nearly zero onset overpotential for HER and HOR, with overpotentials of 12-19 mV to reach −10 mA cm−2 in neutral and alkaline electrolytes. It outperforms Pt/C catalysts loaded on NF under similar conditions. The catalyst exhibits robust long-term durability with negligible degradation after 5000-10000 CV cycles and stable operation over 50 h at −10 mA cm−2 and 10 h at −100 mA cm−2. Faradaic efficiency is close to 100%.

Ni3N/Ni/NF maintains stable anodic current density of ~8 mA cm−2 for HOR over 24 h in H2-saturated 0.1 M KOH, with superior CO tolerance compared to Pt/NF. The catalyst shows only 3.5% decrease in HOR current density in 2% CO/H2 mixture, while Pt/NF decreases by ~63%.
The exchange current density (j) was calculated by dividing i_0 by the geometric electrode area, where i_0 can be obtained from the slope of the linear fitting in the micropolarization region.

The cathode and anode were separated by a porous solid electrolyte (PSE) layer, with gas diffusion layer (GDL) electrodes coated with CO2RR and HOR catalysts used as cathode and anode respectively, and anion and cation exchange membranes (AEM and CEM) in close contact with catalysts for efficient ion transportation (doc_1861_chunk_169325, Results and discussion).

Electrochemical CO2 reduction reaction (CO2RR) selectively reduces CO2 to formate ions on the cathode catalyst; on the anode side, hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) releases protons; formate and protons migrate through membranes into the PSE layer and recombine to form molecular formic acid (doc_1861_chunk_169325, Results and discussion).

The nBuLi-Bi catalyst showed high intrinsic CO2RR-to-formate activity with maximal Faradaic efficiency (FE) of ~97% and formate partial current densities >440 mA cm-2 in neutral pH electrolyte (doc_1861_chunk_169333, Results and discussion).

The cathode side was supplied with humidified CO2 gas (typically 50 s.c.c.m.) and the anode side was supplied with humidified H2 gas (typically 50 s.c.c.m.) for the hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) (doc_1861_chunk_169351, Results and discussion).
The nBuLi-Bi catalyst achieved formate partial current densities up to 460 mA cm-2 with maximal Faradaic efficiency of ~97% in neutral electrolyte (doc_1861_chunk_169333, Results and discussion).

In the all-solid-state PSE cell with DI water flow, pure formic acid solutions up to 2.85 M (~12.8 wt%) were obtained with formic acid FE above 80% at current densities up to 421 mA cm-2; maximal formic acid partial current density was 440 mA cm-2 at 2.19 V cell voltage (doc_1861_chunk_169336, Results and discussion).

Using humidified N2 gas flow to carry formic acid vapor, condensed formic acid solutions up to 11.0 wt% (2.4 M) were obtained at 200 mA cm-2 with ~72% FE; lowering N2 flow to 10 s.c.c.m. increased concentration to 60.5 wt% (14.8 M); dry N2 flow enabled nearly 100 wt% pure formic acid product (doc_1861_chunk_169341, Results and discussion).

The all-solid-state cell demonstrated stable continuous production of ~0.1 M pure formic acid solution for 100 h at 30 mA cm-2 with DI water flow, and ~0.35 M pure formic acid for 30 h with humidified N2 gas flow (doc_1861_chunk_169336, Results and discussion; doc_1861_chunk_169342, Results and discussion).

At high formic acid concentrations within the solid electrolyte layer using DI water flow, Faradaic efficiency dramatically decreased due to increased reverse reaction rate and increased crossover to the anode (doc_1861_chunk_169339, Results and discussion).

Degradation of anion exchange membrane (AEM) during long-term operation at high current density was observed, possibly due to partial neutralization by CO2 gas and increased hydroxide ion concentration, leading to decreased ionic conductivity and increased cell voltage (doc_1861_chunk_169337, Results and discussion).

The catalysts catalyze the hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) efficiently in alkaline electrolytes by synergistic optimization of hydrogen adsorption on Ni sites and hydroxyl adsorption on Mo (or W) sites, promoting the Volmer reaction step. The HOR mechanism involves optimized hydrogen binding energy (HBE) and hydroxyl binding energy (OHBE) on the alloy surface. (chunk_id: doc_1864_chunk_169592, section_title: )

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations show that MoNi4 and WNi4 alloys have weakened hydrogen binding energy compared to pure Ni, similar to Pt, and enhanced hydroxyl adsorption compared to Pt, which promotes the Volmer step in alkaline HOR. CO-stripping experiments indicate enhanced OH binding energy on MoNi4 and WNi4 compared to Pt/C. (chunk_id: doc_1864_chunk_169620, section_title: Results)

The HOR activity was tested in H2-saturated 0.1 M KOH alkaline electrolyte using a three-electrode setup with a rotating disk electrode (RDE). Catalyst loading was optimized to 0.5 mg cm-2 on glassy carbon electrode. CO tolerance was evaluated by introducing 20,000 ppm CO in the hydrogen feed. (chunk_id: doc_1864_chunk_169605, section_title: Results)

The MoNi4 catalyst exhibits a high apparent exchange current density of 3.41 mA cm-2, 1.4 times higher than commercial Pt/C catalyst (2.47 mA cm-2) and much higher than freshly synthesized Ni (0.19 mA cm-2). At 50 mV overpotential, the geometric kinetic current density is 33.8 mA cm-2, representing 105- and 2.8-fold increases compared to Ni and Pt/C, respectively. The catalysts show low onset potential near 0 V vs RHE and reach diffusion-limiting current at >0.05 V. Activation energy for HOR on MoNi4 is 18.59 kJ mol-1, lower than Pt/C (20.07 kJ mol-1) and WNi4 (27.42 kJ mol-1), indicating superior kinetics. The catalysts demonstrate excellent stability: after 2000 accelerated stability test cycles, MoNi4 loses only 0.25 mA cm-2 at 50 mV overpotential, and WNi4 loses 0.41 mA cm-2. Chronoamperometry at 60 mV overpotential for 20 h shows stable current density for MoNi4 with no obvious decay, outperforming Pt/C which retains only 57% of initial current due to Pt nanoparticle agglomeration. Postmortem characterization confirms morphology and structure retention of MoNi4. (chunk_id: doc_1864_chunk_169613, section_title: Results)

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis shows that after calcination, nanoparticles are partially oxidized. Ru is reduced to metallic form after HOR test. Ni is present as metallic Ni(0) and Ni(II) oxides; after calcination Ni is oxidized and after HOR test Ni(II) oxides predominate on the surface, indicating surface Ni oxides exist during HOR while interior remains RuNi alloy (doc_1867_chunk_169814, doc_1867_chunk_169815, section_title: Results)

The hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) involves adsorption of H2 on catalyst surface forming adsorbed hydrogen (H*), followed by electron transfer and desorption as H+. The intrinsic hydrogen binding energy (HBE) between H* and metal is critical for HOR activity. Water binding energy also influences HOR/HER activity. The apparent hydrogen binding energy (HBEapp) is the net effect of catalyst hydrogen binding and water adsorption. Optimized HBEapp close to zero eV yields highest HOR activity. Ru7Ni3/C shows the lowest Hupd peak potential indicating HBEapp closest to zero and highest HOR activity.
