IrNi/C-HT catalysts exhibited high catalytic activity for OER, HER, and HOR with remarkable reversibility after cycling between these reactions, maintaining high HER and HOR activity after OER operation. IrNi/C-LT showed irreversible loss of HER and HOR activity after OER (chunk_id: doc_1886_chunk_171451, section_title: Results)

In PEM fuel cell fuel starvation tests, IrNi/C-HT showed only 9.2% performance degradation after fuel starvation, while IrNi/C-LT showed 26.9% degradation and commercial Pt/C showed 37.2% degradation due to carbon corrosion (chunk_id: doc_1886_chunk_171471, section_title: Results)

In water electrolyzer reverse voltage tests, IrNi/C-HT maintained stable cell voltage after multiple polarity reversals, while IrNi/C-LT showed gradual voltage increase, indicating better reversibility and durability of IrNi/C-HT under real device conditions (chunk_id: doc_1886_chunk_171472, section_title: Results)

Voltage reversal in water electrolyzers and fuel starvation in PEM fuel cells induce large positive potentials on hydrogen electrodes, causing severe degradation including irreversible oxidation of Ir-based catalysts to amorphous IrOx, which leads to loss of multifunctional catalytic properties and system durability (chunk_id: doc_1886_chunk_171439, section_title: )

IrNi/C-LT with low crystallinity forms thick amorphous IrNiOx layers that are irreversible and lead to poor HER and HOR performance after OER, indicating failure due to irreversible oxidation and loss of catalytic activity (chunk_id: doc_1886_chunk_171459, section_title: Results)

At the anode, hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) generates protons which combine with formate ions transported through the AEM to form formic acid at the CEM/AEM interface and within the PCEM pores. The cathode reduces CO2 to formate ions which traverse the AEM.
Formic acid oxidation occurs at the anode, competing with HOR, and can increase anode overpotential and cell voltage. Suppressing formic acid oxidation improves energy efficiency.

Increasing AEM thickness from 25 to 80 µm reduces formic acid back diffusion to the cathode, increasing cathode pH and reducing hydrogen evolution, but increases ohmic losses. An 80 µm AEM thickness enabled >75% Faradaic efficiency to formic acid at <2 V and 300 mA/cm2.
The PCEM/AEM MEA configuration achieves >75% Faradaic efficiency to formic acid at <2 V and 300 mA/cm2 in a 25 cm2 cell.
A 55-hour stability test at 200 mA/cm2 shows stable Faradaic efficiency and cell voltage with a degradation rate of 0.6 mV/hr.

Increasing anode DI water flow rate reduces formic acid concentration in the anode effluent from 0.27 to 0.08 mol/L, decreases formic acid oxidation, and lowers cell voltage to just below 1.7 V at 200 mA/cm2.

The cell contains a Ni(OH)2/NiOOH mediator sandwiched by a CO2RR gas diffusion electrode (GDE) and a HOR GDE to decouple CO2RR and HOR while eliminating sluggish oxygen catalysis. The cell operates in two alternating steps: Step 1 reduces CO2 to CO or formate at the CO2RR GDE while oxidizing Ni(OH)2 to NiOOH; Step 2 uses Ni electrode and HOR GDE for energy harvesting, reducing NiOOH back to Ni(OH)2 and oxidizing H2.

Zn nanosheets electrodeposited on Cu foam form the catalyst for CO2-to-CO conversion, with electrodeposition time controlling nanosheet thickness and size. Bi2O3 porous nanospheres synthesized hydrothermally serve as catalysts for CO2-to-formate conversion. A gradient functional layer with carbon and PTFE phases is fabricated on Zn-Cu foam to form the GDE, enabling effective CO2 transport and electrolyte management.

The cell assembly includes a CO2RR GDE (1×1 cm2), a Ni(OH)2/NiOOH mediator supported on Ni foam (2×2 cm2), and a Pt/C GDE (1×1 cm2) for HOR. A PEEK frame separates the CO2RR GDE and mediator, with a Hg/HgO reference electrode placed in between. The mediator and HOR GDE are separated by a 130-μm-thick porous separator soaked in 1 M KOH.

Ni(OH)2 oxidation reaction (NiOR) has a lower onset potential and Tafel slope compared to oxygen evolution reaction (OER), indicating more favorable kinetics. NiOR is a single-electron transfer process, whereas OER involves a concerted four-electron-proton transfer and gas evolution, causing higher overpotential and sluggish kinetics.

The Ni(OH)2/NiOOH mediator prevents carbon loss and HOR catalyst poisoning by migrated CO2RR products. Without the mediator, HOR catalyst (Pt) suffers CO poisoning from CO2RR products migrating through electrolyte or membrane, causing performance degradation.

Both NiOR and NiRR of Ni(OH)2/NiOOH electrode, as well as the OER of Co3O4 on Ni foam (1 × 1 cm2) were studied in a three-electrode system. 1 M KOH solution was used as the electrolyte, a platinum plate (1 × 1 cm2) was used as the counter electrode, and a Hg/HgO electrode was used as the reference electrode. LSV and CV was performed at a scan rate of 1 mV s−1. The Tafel slope was calculated using the Tafel equation: η = b log(j) + a, where η, b, and j represent the overpotential, Tafel slope, and current density, respectively.

During the CO2RR test, CO2 was purged into the cathodic compartment, the measured outlet flow rate was ~27 mL min−1. EIS was recorded at frequencies ranging from 105 Hz to 0.01 Hz. ECSA was estimated by measuring an electric double layer capacitance from the scan-rate-dependent CV at various scan rates (20, 40, 60, 80, 100 and 120 mV s−1) in an Ar-saturated 0.5 M Na2SO4 electrolyte. The potential window was selected from −0.57 to −0.46 V vs. SCE. The capacitance (Cdl) was estimated by plotting the difference of charging current density versus the scan rate.

CO2 is fed to the cathode side where it is reduced to CO or formate. Hydrogen gas is fed to the anode side during Step 2 for hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR). The electrolyte is typically 1 M KOH for CO2RR and 6 M KOH for HOR to minimize polarization losses.

The cell achieves high selectivity for CO (up to 81.9% FE at 150 mA cm−2) and formate (up to 95.3% FE at 150 mA cm−2) at voltages below 0.9 V at 50 mA cm−2. The cell voltage for Step 1 is 0.15–0.23 V lower than conventional processes due to the kinetic advantage of NiOR over OER.

The maximum power density for Step 2 (HOR) reaches 221 mW cm−2 with voltage efficiency higher than 95%. The system shows stable operation over 100 hours with trivial voltage degradation and no catalyst degradation observed in SEM or FTIR analyses.

Cells can be stacked by combining hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) gas diffusion electrodes into a bipolar gas diffusion electrode (BPGDE), which recirculates hydrogen with near unity efficiency (chunk_id: doc_2044_chunk_187821, section_title: Results and discussion).

The cell uses hydrogen redox reactions for H+ and OH– production, specifically the hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) at the anode and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) at the cathode (chunk_id: doc_2044_chunk_187793, section_title: Results and discussion).

The electrolytes used are composed of NaCl and Na2SO4 salts, with mixed NaCl + Na2SO4 electrolytes providing the best performance by outcompeting OH– transport with Cl– and binding H+ as HSO4– (chunk_id: doc_2044_chunk_187793, section_title: Results and discussion).

The system tolerates polyvalent cation impurities (e.g., Mg and Si at ~300 µM) with periodic reverse polarization to remove scale deposits and maintain voltage stability over long-term operation (chunk_id: doc_2044_chunk_187817, section_title: Results and discussion).

The bipolar gas diffusion electrode (BPGDE) stack operated at 100 mA cm–2 and 0.1 mL min–1 cm–2 flow rate with mixed electrolyte showed stable voltage (~2.03 V) and current efficiency of 73% over 25 hours, with near unity hydrogen utilization efficiency (chunk_id: doc_2044_chunk_187823, section_title: Results and discussion).

The strong interaction between Ru NPs and WCx support results in electron-deficient Ru sites, optimizing adsorption of intermediates (H* and OH*) and enhancing both hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) performances in alkaline media.

The Ru-H bond energy is reduced due to strong interaction between Ru NPs and WCx substrate, weakening hydrogen binding energy (HBE) and enhancing hydroxyl binding energy (OHBE), optimizing adsorption of Had and OHad intermediates, boosting HOR/HER performance in alkaline conditions.

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations indicate that Ru-NPs/WCx configuration exhibits significant HOR performance by optimizing both HBE and OHBE mechanisms, facilitating dissociation of H2 and adsorption of OH species, which co-exist in alkaline HOR/HER processes.

Operando electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) shows Ru-NPs/WCx has smaller semicircle and accelerated charge transfer compared to Pt/C, indicating rapid electro/proton transfers and faster reaction dynamics for HOR/HER.

The weakening of *H adsorption and enhancement of *OH adsorption on Ru-NPs/WC_x due to electron transfer from Ru to WC_x optimizes the HOR/HER processes, boosting performance via both HBE and OHBE pathways.

The Ru-NPs/WCx catalyst is used as anode in anion-exchange membrane fuel cells (AEMFCs) and alkaline anion-exchange membrane water electrolyzers (AEMWEs), demonstrating high performance and durability.

The Ru-NPs/WCx-based H2-air AEMFC delivers high peak power density and mass activity at low total metal loadings, with more than 210 h durability. The AEMWE with Ru-NPs/WCx cathode requires only 1.73 V to reach industrial current density of 1 A cm-2 and maintains over 150 h at 500 mA cm-2 without decay.

The Ru-NPs/WCx-based AEMFC shows high stability with only 0.9 mV h-1 voltage decay over 210 h at 250 mA cm-2 in H2-air (CO2-free), outperforming Ru/C which shows fast decline. The catalyst maintains structure and surface chemical states after durability tests, with strong antioxidation of WCx substrate.

The Ru-NPs/WCx catalyst also shows high CO tolerance in AEMFC operation with 100 ppm CO in H2 feed, maintaining current density and peak power density similar to CO-free conditions, demonstrating advantage for practical application with crude H2 feedstock.

The HOR performance was evaluated in H2-saturated 0.1 M KOH electrolyte using rotating disk electrode (RDE) setup. The Ru-NPs/WCx catalyst shows higher HOR activity than Pt/C and Ru/C, with current density of 2.46 mA cm-2 at 50 mV, and stable performance up to 0.28 V vs RHE.

The back side of the gas diffusion electrode (GDE) was pre-treated with a 10 wt% PTFE solution to mitigate potential electrolyte flooding, by air-spraying 4 mL of PTFE solution onto a 10 cm2 carbon paper, followed by calcination at 330 °C for 30 minutes with a heating rate of 5 °C/min.

For the long-term stability tests of the ECR//HOR electrolyzer, flow cells with electrode areas of either 0.5 cm × 2 cm or 2 cm × 2 cm were used, with electrolyte flow layer thicknesses of 2 mm and 3 mm respectively, and electrolyte volumes of 500 mL and 1000 mL circulated at flow rates of 8 mL/min and 5 mL/min respectively, refreshed every 24 hours.

The membrane-free CO2 hydrogenation electrolyzer couples CO2 reduction and hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) to maintain stable electrolyte pH and achieve high Faradaic efficiency for HCOOH production (chunk_id: doc_2051_chunk_188638, section_title: ).

Hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) at the anode replaces oxygen evolution reaction (OER) to prevent oxidation of ECR products and reduce cell voltage in membrane-free electrolyzer (chunk_id: doc_2051_chunk_188666, section_title: Results).

The ECR performance of Bi0.5Ag0.5 was evaluated in acidic, neutral, and alkaline solutions using 0.5 M K2SO4 + H2SO4 (pH = 1.8 ± 0.02), 0.5 M K2SO4 (pH = 7.43 ± 0.08), and 1 M KOH (pH = 14 ± 0.05) as electrolytes respectively, with CO2 gas delivered into the cathodic compartment at 20 sccm.

HOR polarization curves under different H2 flow rates and H2/Ar ratios were measured in a three-electrode system with electrode areas of 1 cm × 1 cm; polarization curves were performed from 0 to 1.6 V (vs. RHE) at a scan rate of 20 mV/s in 0.5 M K2SO4 (pH = 1.8) electrolyte with 8 mL/min flow rate and 100% iR compensation applied.
Chronopotentiometry tests were carried out at current densities of −100, −200, −300, and −400 mA cm−2 to compare the ECR performance of different catalysts.

For the integrated ECR//HOR and HER//AEM//MOR electrolyzer, electrolysis was performed at a current density of 100 mA cm−2 at the HER//AEM//MOR electrolyzer for 20 min to produce enough H2 and purge air from the catholyte and electrolyte container, followed by supplying H2 into the ECR//HOR electrolyzer.

Membrane-free electrolyzer design bypasses limitations in proton transfer caused by membrane, maintaining stable catholyte pH and high proton transfer efficiency close to 100% (chunk_id: doc_2051_chunk_188666, section_title: Results).
Membrane resistance in membrane-based electrolyzers increases cell voltage and contributes to catholyte pH increase and salt precipitation (chunk_id: doc_2051_chunk_188668, section_title: Results).

The reactor is a three-chamber electrochemical cell with a porous solid electrolyte (PSE) layer separated by a cation exchange membrane (CEM) and a proton exchange membrane (PEM) between cathode and anode. The PSE layer contains dense but water- and gas-permeable polymers functionalized with cation-conducting sulfonate groups. The cathode uses Pt/C catalyst on hydrophilic carbon cloth for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), and the anode uses Pt/C on hydrophobic carbon paper for hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR). The cathode side is supplied with deionized water, the middle chamber is fed with (bi)carbonate solutions, and the anode side is supplied with H2 gas. The CEM is Nafion N2100TX or PT

The reactor design principle involves performing HER and HOR redox reactions to split NaHCO3/Na2CO3 solutions in the PSE layer, regenerating NaOH absorbent in the catholyte and high-purity CO2 gas in the PSE layer. Protons generated at the anode/PEM interface are driven into the middle layer to replace sodium ions that move across the CEM into the cathode chamber. The PSE layer nucleates CO2 bubbles and minimizes ohmic loss, especially under low salinity for low energy consumption.

The electrochemical reactions involve hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) at the cathode and hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) at the anode. Protons generated at the anode are driven into the middle PSE layer to acidify (bi)carbonate ions, releasing high-purity CO2 gas. Sodium ions migrate across the CEM into the cathode chamber to form NaOH absorbent. This redox electrolysis enables continuous conversion of (bi)carbonate solutions back into CO2 gas and alkaline solutions without chemical consumption or by-products.

Switching the anode reaction from HOR to oxygen evolution reaction (OER) allows net hydrogen generation at the cathode with an extra cell voltage input (~2 V onset, ~1.5 V higher than HER/HOR). This configuration enables combined water electrolysis and carbon capture in a single electrolyser device, potentially lowering capital and operation costs. Changing half-cell reactions does not affect the Na+ ion transport number, which remains above 80% across a wide range of cell currents.

The reactor uses a cation exchange membrane (CEM) close to the cathode (Nafion N2100TX or PTFE fabric-reinforced Nafion) and a proton exchange membrane (PEM) close to the anode (Nafion-117). The CEM selectively transports Na+ ions, while the PEM transports protons (H+). The PSE layer is sandwiched between these membranes and contains sulfonate-functionalized polymers that conduct cations and facilitate CO2 bubble nucleation. Membrane choice affects Na+ ion transport number; for example, using Nafion-117 on the cathode side decreases Na+ transport due to enhanced H+ conduction.

The feed to the reactor consists of aqueous (bi)carbonate solutions such as NaHCO3 or Na2CO3 pumped continuously into the porous solid electrolyte (PSE) layer. The cathode side is supplied with deionized water to remove crossing-over Na+ ions and minimize mass transport impacts. The anode side is supplied with hydrogen gas at 30 sccm for the hydrogen oxidation reaction. In practical operation, electrolyte recirculation mode is used with volumes of 20 ml to 400 ml for the middle PSE layer and catholyte to enable batch cycling of CO2 regeneration.

The reactor achieves high Na+ ion transport numbers (~90%) across a wide range of current densities and cell voltages when fed with 0.5 M NaHCO3 solution. The Na+ transport number slightly decreases with lower bicarbonate concentration but remains high (80% at 0.1 M, 70% at 0.01 M). CO2 regeneration rates reach 6.78 mmol cm−2 h−1 at 200 mA cm−2 with 90% Na+ transport number. Industrially relevant current densities up to 1.5 A cm−2 were achieved with a Na+ transport number of 65%, corresponding to ~18 mmol cm−2 h−1 CO2 generation rate. Energy consumption starts from 50 kJ mol_CO2−1 for bicarbonate and increases with regeneration rate. The reactor shows long-term stability over 100 hours with

A rapid increase in cell voltage to ~4 V indicates depletion of Na+ ions in the middle PSE layer during continuous electrolysis, serving as an indicator to stop the regeneration step to maintain good Na+ transport number and energy efficiency. Slight volume increase in catholyte due to osmosis effects causes a slight underestimation of Na+ transport number. Gas leakage in the reactor system causes the Faradaic efficiency of CO2 gas collection to be slightly lower than the Na+ transport number. No other failure modes or degradation were reported over 100 hours of operation.
Platinum is electrodeposited onto SSCs to create active hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) electrodes (Pt/SSC).
Hydrogen oxidation at the anode produces protons of controlled thermodynamic activity, avoiding solvent oxidation and enabling continuous ammonia production.
Hydrogen oxidation current densities up to 25 mA cm−2 with near-unity Faradaic efficiency are achieved in non-aqueous solvents using Pt/SSC electrodes.

Nitrogen reduction rates are substantially enhanced using SSC cathodes compared to flooded electrodes, achieving ammonia partial current densities of 8.8 ± 1.4 mA cm−2 and Faradaic efficiencies up to 47.5 ± 4%.

Coupling hydrogen oxidation at Pt/SSC anode with nitrogen reduction at SSC cathode enables an electrochemical Haber–Bosch reactor producing ammonia from nitrogen and water-splitting-derived hydrogen at ambient conditions.

Hydrogen oxidation current densities up to 25 mA cm−2 with near-unity Faradaic efficiency are achieved using Pt/SSC electrodes in THF and propylene carbonate electrolytes, an order of magnitude higher than flooded electrodes.
Ammonia partial current density of 8.8 ± 1.4 mA cm−2 and Faradaic efficiencies of 35 ± 6% (rate-optimized) and 47.5 ± 4% (FE-optimized) are obtained using SSC cathodes for nitrogen reduction.
The electrochemical Haber–Bosch reactor produces ammonia at ambient conditions with Faradaic efficiency of 30 ± 2% when coupled with water electrolysis as hydrogen source.
Solvent decomposition at the anode due to tetrahydrofuran oxidation limits continuous lithium-mediated ammonia production when hydrogen oxidation is not used.

Current state-of-the-art catalysts are the result of carefully optimizing structure–performance relationships including Pt particle accessibility, dispersion, carbon pore morphology, ionomer structure, and ink formulation with respect to solvent and water content.

In PEMFCs, hydrogen is oxidized at the anode (HOR) producing protons and electrons, and oxygen is reduced at the cathode (ORR) forming water. In PEMWEs, these reactions proceed in reverse: hydrogen is produced at the cathode (HER) and oxygen evolves at the anode (OER).
The OER is catalyzed by Ir oxide or Ru oxide based electrocatalysts in PEMWEs, while Pt or Pt-based alloys catalyze HOR and HER in PEMFCs and PEMWEs respectively.

The HOR/HER reactions in acidic systems are extremely fast with exchange current densities around 100 mA per cm2 platinum, but RDE measurements underestimate these kinetics due to mass transport limitations.

OER catalyst activities measured in liquid electrolytes using RDEs yield estimated activities comparable to MEA measurements, but stability measurements in RDEs show lifetimes three to four orders of magnitude lower than in MEAs, partly due to oxygen bubble formation blocking active sites.

ORR catalyst activity trends obtained from RDE measurements generally hold across MEA set-ups, but absolute mass activities are lower in MEAs. Pre-leaching Pt alloy catalysts improves MEA performance and reduces discrepancies.
The oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) catalyst kinetics can be improved by optimizing transport resistance, which impacts cell voltage at high current densities.

RDEs provide a quick and widely accessible testing tool for electrocatalyst activity and durability, allowing rapid screening of small catalyst quantities, but have limitations in mass transport and contamination effects.
MEA testing is more complex and time-consuming but closely resembles actual application conditions, providing more reliable data on catalyst performance and durability.

OER catalyst activities from RDE measurements match MEA values, but RDE stability tests show rapid reversible degradation due to oxygen bubble formation, which does not occur in MEAs, making MEA testing preferable for stability.

GDLs can be single-layer or dual-layer. Single-layer GDLs are macroporous carbon substrates with hydrophobic agents like PTFE to control hydrophobicity and electrical conductivity. Dual-layer GDLs include a microporous layer (MPL) between the macroporous layer and catalyst layer, improving water management, hydrophobicity, and structural integrity. (chunk_id: doc_2133_chunk_196959, section_title: 2.3. GDE fabrication and structure)

Ink-based methods for CL fabrication involve catalyst powders, solvents, and ionic binders (e.g., Nafion). Deposition methods include air-brushing, drop-casting, and hand-painting, with air-brushing producing thicker, more uniform CLs and better performance. (chunk_id: doc_2133_chunk_196965, section_title: 2.3. GDE fabrication and structure)

Ink-free methods such as sputtering and electrodeposition allow uniform catalyst deposition without binders, enabling thin, stable catalyst layers with strong interaction with GDLs. Sputtering can be applied directly on PTFE-based GDLs. (chunk_id: doc_2133_chunk_196983, section_title: 2.3. GDE fabrication and structure)

Electrodeposition comprises three main methods: constant current (galvanostatic), constant potential (potentiostatic), and pulse electrodeposition, where electrodeposition stops for a designated duration and then restarts. The catalytic performance with pulse electrodeposition is better than that with the other two methods. Electrodeposition also tunes the morphology of the CL, altering the product selectivity. It is speculated that during the off-time in pulse electrodeposition, the ion concentration around the electrode is replenished via ion diffusion from the electroplating bulk, which is beneficial for the formation of uniform particles on the substrate. Alternatively, the catalyst can be directly deposited on the ion-exchange membrane, and then a carbon-based GDL is inserted to the cell together with the MEA (membrane-based MEA fabrication). This method is more commonly used due to the superior CL/ion-exchange membrane interface. MEA is prone to swelling due to hydration during electrochemical reactions and CL directly attached to the ion-exchange membrane shows better stability if swelling happens. To improve the connection between the deposited CL and ion-exchange membrane, the membrane is usually hot-pressed at around 120 °C. The article presents a comprehensive review covering recent research progress in value-added NH3 production through photo-, electro-, photo/electrochemical, lithium-mediated, calcium-mediated and other advanced green pathways, including material design and preparation from different nitrogen sources, structural characterization, and performance evaluation.

The article summarizes the fundamental mechanistic understanding, catalytic descriptors, and current advances, along with the various catalysts used for nitrogen resource conversion strategies and their structure–activity relationships.
A porphyrin-based metal–organic framework (PMOF) was developed containing atomically dispersed Al and Fe metal centers to promote the adsorption and activation of N2 (Al-PMOF(Fe)).

A trinuclear Fe-O cluster-based MOF (MIL-100(Fe)) catalyst with dual defects was synthesized using a non-thermal plasma-assisted strategy, creating abundant exposed, coordinatively unsaturated Fe sites to inject more d-electrons into N2 molecules.
Fe-doped W18O49 nanowires mixed with black phosphorus quantum dots formed P-Fe/W18O49, which increased N2 reduction ability and suppressed charge recombination.
A GDY@Fe–B heterojunction photocatalyst was produced, changing the coordination environment and valence state of Fe atoms to improve photocatalytic activity.

A three-electrode flow electrolyzer was designed with a PTFE spacer for catholyte flow and a catalyst-supported gas diffusion electrode attached to an anion exchange membrane for anolyte flow, enabling enhanced mass transport and reduced ohmic loss.

Electrocatalytic hydrogenation (ECH) of 5-(hydroxymethyl)furfural (HMF) to 2,5-bis(hydroxymethyl)furan (BHMF) was performed on OD-Ag with high activity and up to 98.2% selectivity. The anodic oxygen evolution reaction (OER) was replaced by TEMPO-mediated oxidation of HMF to 2,5-furandicarboxylic acid (FDCA), reducing cell voltage from ~7.5 V to ~2.0 V at 10 mA and increasing energy efficiency more than fourfold.
The anodic hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) on Pt/C was used as an alternative anodic reaction paired with ECH of HMF on OD-Ag, further reducing the cell voltage to ~0.85 V at 10 mA.
Thermodynamic calculations show that replacing anodic OER with ECO of HMF to FDCA lowers the theoretical cell potential from 1.11 V to 0.33 V, reducing energy input for paired electrolysis.
An anion exchange membrane (Tokuyama A201) was used to separate anode and cathode compartments in both H-type and flow cells, enabling paired electrolysis with minimized crossover.
For ECH–HOR paired electrolysis, hydrogen gas was supplied at the anode for hydrogen oxidation reaction on Pt/C electrode.

OD-Ag electrode showed 240 mV positive shift in onset potential and 2–3 times higher current density for HMF reduction compared to Ag foil, with BHMF selectivity above 90% over a broad potential range (-1.05 to -1.35 V vs Ag/AgCl) and long-term operational stability.

ECH–ECO paired electrolysis in flow cell reduced steady-state cell voltage from ~7.5 V (H-type cell) to ~2.0 V at 10 mA, with combined faradaic efficiency of 163% for BHMF and FDCA, and over fourfold increase in energy efficiency (24.5% vs 5.7%).
ECH–HOR paired electrolysis achieved cell voltage of only ~0.85 V at 10 mA with BHMF selectivity of 82.3%, offering a low energy cost pathway for BHMF production.
No significant performance drop or stability issues were observed during prolonged electrolysis on OD-Ag in flow cell, indicating high durability and robustness.

Membranes critically influence water availability and electrochemical reaction environment in full electrolyzers; common types include diaphragm separators, PEMs, AEMs, BPMs, and ISMs. Membranes tailored for neutral pH face challenges like reduced ionic conductivity and contamination risks, requiring balance between ion transport, contaminant rejection, and stability (chunk_id=doc_2189_chunk_202292).

Surface-modified membranes (e.g., Perfluorosulfonic acid with graphene oxide) and functionalized nanomaterials improve ion selectivity, resist fouling, and prevent degradation, enhancing membrane stability and selectivity in paired electrolysis systems (chunk_id=doc_2189_chunk_202298, doc_2189_chunk_202299).

Paired electrolysis involves simultaneous anodic and cathodic valorization, with anodic reactions including organic electrosynthesis, plastic upcycling, biomass valorization, and commodity chemical oxidation, while cathodic reactions include hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), CO2 reduction, and nitrogen/nitrate reduction (chunk_id=doc_2189_chunk_202279, doc_2189_chunk_202302, doc_2189_chunk_202304).

Standard oxidation potentials of small molecules like alcohols and amines are lower than OER, but slow kinetics and catalyst surface properties often require higher anodic potentials; at industrial current densities, anodic electrode potentials can reach 3–5 V vs. RHE, with total cell voltages up to 6–8 V (chunk_id=doc_2189_chunk_202312, doc_2189_chunk_202314).

Catalyst optimization (surface area, morphology, active site distribution) and functional group modifications (–OH, –COOH, –SO3H) enhance reactivity, reduce overpotentials, and improve selectivity (chunk_id=doc_2189_chunk_202316).

Membranes such as diaphragm separators, PEMs, AEMs, BPMs, and ISMs are used in water electrolysis; membranes for neutral pH operation face challenges including reduced ionic conductivity and contamination risks, requiring balance between ion transport, contaminant rejection, and stability (chunk_id=doc_2189_chunk_202292).

Surface-modified membranes (e.g., Perfluorosulfonic acid with graphene oxide) and functionalized nanomaterials improve ion selectivity, resist fouling, and prevent degradation, enhancing membrane stability and selectivity in paired electrolysis systems (chunk_id=doc_2189_chunk_202298, doc_2189_chunk_202299).

Industrial-level current densities (>1 A/cm2) are targeted; recent advancements achieve Faradaic efficiencies between 60% and 96% at 0.2–2.0 A/cm2; biomass valorization and organic electrosynthesis require high selectivity, product purity, and long-term stability (chunk_id=doc_2189_chunk_202337, doc_2189_chunk_202338, doc_2189_chunk_202339).

Plastic upcycling catalysts achieve high efficiency and stability at industrial current densities (e.g., Pd–Ni foam catalyst converts PET with 100% efficiency at 400 mA/cm2) (chunk_id=doc_2189_chunk_202341).

Energy efficiency is improved by reducing overpotentials and optimizing catalysts; ascorbic acid electrooxidation reached 2 A/cm2 at 1.1 V with nearly 100% efficiency; HMF oxidation achieved 200% FE at 100 mA/cm2 with significantly lower energy consumption than conventional water electrolysis (chunk_id=doc_2189_chunk_202342).

The cathode and anode were separated by a porous solid electrolyte (PSE) layer, where electrochemically generated formate and proton were recombined to form molecular formic acid. Gas diffusion layer (GDL) electrodes coated with CO2RR and HOR catalysts were used as cathode and anode to improve mass transfer of CO2 and H2. Both catalysts were in close contact with anion and cation exchange membranes (AEM and CEM), respectively, for efficient ion transportation.

Electrochemical CO2 reduction reaction (CO2RR) converts CO2 to formate ions on a selective CO2RR catalyst at the cathode; at the anode, hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) releases protons from H2 gas stream. Electrochemically generated HCOO− and H+ move across AEM and CEM into the PSE layer and recombine to form HCOOH molecule.

The cathode side was supplied with humidified CO2 gas; the anode side was supplied with humidified H2 gas for HOR. For product removal, either deionized (DI) water flow or inert N2 gas flow was used through the PSE layer. DI water flow collects formic acid in solution but dilutes concentration; inert N2 gas flow carries formic acid vapors for high-concentration product collection via cold condensation.

Using humidified N2 gas flow at 100 s.c.c.m. and 200 mA cm−2 current, condensed formic acid solution reached 11.0 wt.% (2.4 M) with ~72% Faradaic efficiency. Lowering N2 flow to 10 s.c.c.m. increased concentration to 60.5 wt.% (14.8 M). Using dry N2 flow, nearly 100 wt.% pure formic acid vapor was obtained, demonstrating ultra-pure product generation without downstream purification.

The nBuLi-Bi catalyst achieved formate partial current densities >440 mA cm−2 with maximal Faradaic efficiency ~97% and stability over 100 hours. In the all-solid-state PSE cell with DI water flow, formic acid partial current density reached 440 mA cm−2 at 2.19 V cell voltage, producing 0.62 M pure formic acid solution at 8.21 mmol h−1 cm−2. Stability tests showed 100 h continuous production of ~0.1 M pure formic acid solution at 30 mA cm−2 with stable selectivity.

Using N2 gas flow to carry formic acid vapor, the system achieved high product concentrations while maintaining high selectivity. The all-solid-state cell showed 30 h continuous production of ~0.35 M pure formic acid product with humidified N2 gas flow. The system demonstrated superior catalytic performance compared with existing systems.

At high formic acid concentrations within the solid electrolyte layer using DI water flow, Faradaic efficiency dramatically decreased due to increased reverse reaction rate and increased crossover to the anode. Stability degradation at high current density was mainly caused by degradation of the anion exchange membrane (AEM), which could be partially neutralized by CO2 gas, decreasing ionic conductivity and increasing cell voltage. Dry N2 flow for ultra-pure product may cause gradual degradation of membranes and solid electrolytes without wetting conditions.

The MEA assembly includes a Pt/C anode catalyst spray-coated on the perforated CEM (Nafion NC700, 15 µm thick), with an ionomer-to-carbon ratio of 0.83 and loading of 0.25 mg Pt/cm2. The AEM membrane (PiperION) with thicknesses of 25, 40, or 80 µm is placed on top of the CEM and then the cathode gas diffusion electrode (GDE). The AEM is cut to 7.5×7.5 cm and soaked overnight in 1 M KOH before assembly. PTFE gaskets achieve 18% GDE compression.

At the anode, hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) generates protons supplied to the bipolar membrane interface. At the cathode, CO2 is electrochemically reduced to formate ions which traverse the AEM and combine with protons at the CEM/AEM interface to form formic acid. Formic acid then exits through the perforated CEM and anode flow field.

Formic acid oxidation occurs at the Pt/C anode catalyst, competing with HOR, leading to increased anode overpotential and cell voltage. Formic acid oxidation rate increases with formic acid accumulation at the PCEM/AEM interface and can be mitigated by increasing anode DI water flow rate to reduce formic acid concentration.

The cathode reactions include CO2 reduction to formate/formic acid, hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), and CO formation. The total Faradaic efficiency accounts for formic acid collected, formic acid oxidized at the anode, HER, and CO production.

The key technological advancement is the use of a perforated cation exchange membrane (PCEM) in a forward bias bipolar membrane (BPM) configuration. The PCEM allows formic acid generated at the membrane interface to exit through the anode flow field, preventing salt accumulation and enabling stable operation.

The AEM thickness affects formic acid back diffusion; thicker AEMs (up to 80 µm) reduce back diffusion, increase cathode pH, reduce hydrogen evolution, and improve formic acid Faradaic efficiency, though at the cost of higher ohmic losses.

The PCEM/AEM MEA configuration achieves greater than 75% Faradaic efficiency (FE) to formic acid at less than 2 V cell voltage and 300 mA/cm2 current density in a 25 cm2 cell. Increasing AEM thickness to 80 µm improves FE by reducing formic acid back diffusion.

A 55-hour stability test at 200 mA/cm2 shows stable Faradaic efficiency and cell voltage with a degradation rate of 0.6 mV/hr. Initial formic acid FE is 76.5%, decreasing to 62.7% after 1 hour due to formic acid oxidation, then stabilizing.

Increasing anode DI water flow rate reduces formic acid concentration at the anode from 0.27 to 0.08 mol/L, decreases formic acid oxidation, improves overall FE, and lowers cell voltage to about 1.7 V at 200 mA/cm2.
Single CEM membrane MEA configurations suffer from rapid degradation within two hours at 200 mA/cm2 due to cathode salt accumulation leading to performance loss.

The cell contains a Ni(OH)2/NiOOH mediator sandwiched by a CO2RR gas diffusion electrode (GDE) and a HOR GDE, to decouple CO2RR and HOR while eliminating sluggish oxygen catalysis. The cell operates in two alternating steps: Step 1 for CO2 conversion reducing CO2 to CO or formate at CO2RR GDE with Ni(OH)2 oxidized to NiOOH; Step 2 for energy harvesting where NiOOH is reduced back to Ni(OH)2 and hydrogen is oxidized at the HOR GDE.

A gradient functional layer was fabricated on Zn-Cu foam via a layer-by-layer drop-casting method with varying carbon-PTFE ratios to form the GDE. This gradient microstructure provides gas diffusion channels and ion/electron transport pathways, enabling effective inward CO2 transport and outward electrolyte transport, while ensuring mechanical strength and conductivity.

The cell assembly comprises three electrodes: a CO2RR GDE (1×1 cm2), a Ni(OH)2/NiOOH mediator supported on Ni foam (2×2 cm2), and a Pt/C GDE (1×1 cm2) for HOR. A PEEK frame separates the CO2RR GDE and the mediator, with a Hg/HgO reference electrode placed there. The mediator and HOR GDE are separated by a 130-μm-thick porous separator soaked in 1 M KOH.

We assembled a zero-gap AWE to evaluate the performance in water splitting. Briefly, the hydrothermally formed Co- and Ni-hydroxides on Ni foam was phosphidated in the atmosphere of PH3-containing Ar at 300 °C. The obtained Co-Ni phosphide/spinel oxide hybrid nanostructure was super-aerophobic and active for both HER and OER. The electrolyzer comprised two titanium plates as the current collector. Porous polyethersulfone (PES) with a thickness of 0.12 mm was used as the separator.

Ni(OH)2 oxidation reaction (NiOR) has much lower overpotential loss and faster kinetics than the oxygen evolution reaction (OER). NiOR is a single-electron transfer process, whereas OER involves four-electron-proton transfer and gas evolution, making OER kinetically sluggish. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy shows NiOR has lower charge-transfer resistance than OER catalysts like Co3O4.

In the CO2-to-CO model, Zn nanosheet catalysts show high Faradaic efficiency (FE) for CO formation, with Zn-Cu-500 achieving 85.8% FE at -1.0 V vs RHE and partial current density of ~11 mA cm−2. In the CO2-to-formate model, Bi2O3 porous nanospheres achieve up to 89.0% FE for formate at -0.65 V.

Both NiOR and NiRR of Ni(OH)2/NiOOH electrode, as well as the OER of Co3O4 on Ni foam (1 × 1 cm2) were studied in a three-electrode system. 1 M KOH solution was used as the electrolyte, a platinum plate (1 × 1 cm2) was used as the counter electrode, and a Hg/HgO electrode was used as the reference electrode. LSV and CV was performed at a scan rate of 1 mV s−1. The Tafel slope was calculated using the Tafel equation: η = b log(j) + a, where η, b, and j represent the overpotential, Tafel slope, and current density, respectively.

The CO2 feed is supplied to the cathode gas diffusion electrode where CO2 is reduced to CO or formate. Hydrogen gas is supplied to the anode gas diffusion electrode for the hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) during Step 2. The electrolyte used is weak alkaline (1 M KOH) for CO2RR and stronger alkaline (6 M KOH) for HOR to minimize polarization losses.

The cell achieves high selectivity and stability for CO and formate production with Faradaic efficiencies up to 95.3% and stability over 100 hours at voltages below 0.9 V at 50 mA cm−2. The cell voltage for CO2-to-CO conversion is as low as 0.87 V at 50 mA cm−2, significantly lower than conventional CO2RR cells.

The Ni(OH)2/NiOOH mediator enables operation with voltage efficiencies of 78.4% at 20 mA cm−2 and >95% voltage efficiency for HOR at 20 and 50 mA cm−2. The system shows stable cycling performance over multiple cycles with negligible degradation and maintains CO Faradaic efficiency around 72%.

Cells can be stacked by combining hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) gas diffusion electrodes into a bipolar gas diffusion electrode (BPGDE), which recirculates hydrogen with near unity efficiency (chunk_id: doc_2493_chunk_231591, section_title: Results and discussion).

The cell uses hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) at the anode and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) at the cathode to generate H+ and OH– ions respectively, enabling acid and base production (chunk_id: doc_2493_chunk_231591, section_title: Results and discussion).

Electrolytes composed of NaCl and Na2SO4 salts are used, with mixed NaCl + Na2SO4 electrolytes providing the best performance by outcompeting OH– transport with Cl– and binding H+ as HSO4– (chunk_id: doc_2493_chunk_231591, section_title: Results and discussion).

The cell voltage ranges from 0.90 to 1.54 V with energy efficiencies (EE) from 64% to 19%, and the system tolerates polyvalent cation impurities with periodic reverse polarization to restore voltage (chunk_id: doc_2493_chunk_231615, section_title: Results and discussion).

The bipolar gas diffusion electrode (BPGDE) stack operates at 100 mA cm–2 with a stable voltage of about 2.03 V over 25 hours and near unity hydrogen utilization efficiency, enabling scalable acid-base production (chunk_id: doc_2493_chunk_231621, section_title: Results and discussion).

Post-reaction characterization shows that PdCx/CNT retains its original structure, particle size (~5 nm), and Pd (111) lattice spacing (0.24 nm), with no significant changes in XRD peak positions or intensities, and stable composition and valence states confirmed by XPS. H2-TPD confirms that carbon interstitial atoms occupy subsurface sites, preventing hydrogen lattice absorption, indicating structural and electrochemical stability under operating conditions.

The catalyst ink for the cathode electrode in the PEM electrolyzer was prepared by mixing 20 mg catalyst, 20 μL 1% PTFE emulsion, 2 ml ethanol, and 100 μL 5% ethanol Nafion solution, ultrasonicated for 0.5 hours, then sprayed onto GDL with a mass loading of 0.5 mg cm⁻² (chunk_id: doc_2501_chunk_232572, section_title: Results and Discussion).

The dual mechanism of interstitial carbon infusion enhances charge transfer for HCOOH production, suppresses HER, retains *H as a proton reservoir, and stabilizes *OCHO coverage, improving activity, selectivity, and stability in acidic CO2RR to HCOOH.

Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was used to evaluate CO2RR performance at a sweep rate of 10 mV s⁻¹ with 95% iR correction; durability tests were performed by chronoamperometry; electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted at -0.9 V vs. RHE in CO2 saturated 0.5 M K2SO4 (pH 3 ± 0.03) (chunk_id: doc_2501_chunk_232570, section_title: Results and Discussion).

A proton exchange membrane (PEM) electrolyzer was used for scale-up testing of PdCx/CNT catalysts in acidic electrolyte (0.5 M K2SO4, pH 3). The anodic oxygen evolution reaction was replaced by Pt-Ru black-catalyzed hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR), reducing total voltage, increasing current density at lower overpotential, and avoiding harmful hydrogen peroxide generation, thus enhancing membrane durability and service life.

The PEM electrolyzer with PdCx/CNT catalyst operates stably for 500 hours at 1000 mA cm-2 and 1.8 V cell voltage, maintaining high Faradaic efficiency (~95%) for HCOOH production with minimal decay (~6.8%). The proton-conductive environment combined with *H retention and utilization and suppressed HER kinetics enables efficient and stable HCOOH production.

The CO2 reduction reaction (CO2RR) was performed in acidic electrolyte (0.5 M K2SO4, pH 3 adjusted by H2SO4) saturated with CO2. Rotating disk electrode (RDE) experiments showed enhanced CO2 mass transfer and reduced hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) on PdCx/CNT compared to Pd/CNT. The catalyst exhibits higher current density and improved CO2RR onset potential, with suppressed HER onset potential and reduced H+ reduction current plateau, indicating inhibited HER and enhanced CO2RR activity.

PdCx/CNT catalyst achieves high Faradaic efficiency (FE) for HCOOH (>95%) at 1000 mA cm-2 in acidic electrolyte, with a maximum partial current density for HCOOH of -85.9 mA cm-2 at -0.9 V vs. RHE. The catalyst exhibits a 5-fold increase in FE_HCOOH compared to Pd/CNT and higher cathode energy efficiency (CEE) reaching 64.0%. Tafel slope and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy indicate accelerated CO2RR kinetics and enhanced electron transfer efficiency. Temperature-programmed desorption shows stronger CO2 binding on PdCx/CNT, favorable for reaction.

Long-term stability tests show PdCx/CNT maintains stable current density (~-88.9 mA cm-2) and FE_HCOOH (~94.9%) over 24 hours at -0.9 V vs. RHE in acidic medium. CO stripping tests indicate enhanced CO tolerance with a negative shift in CO oxidation onset potential. Chronoamperometry in CO-saturated electrolyte shows PdCx/CNT maintains stable current, confirming CO poisoning resistance. Post-test characterization confirms structural and compositional stability with negligible Pd dissolution and no hydrogen lattice absorption.

Bipolar membrane electrolyzers (BPMWEs) are composed of a cation exchange layer (CEL) combined with an anion exchange layer (AEL) integrated into a bipolar membrane water electrolyzer device (doc_0060_chunk_005581, RESULTS AND DISCUSSION).

The chlorine oxidation reaction (COR) generates corrosive free chlorine species (Cl2, HOCl, OCl-) at the electrolyzer anode, posing challenges to safety, efficiency, and durability during seawater electrolysis (doc_0060_chunk_005579, INTRODUCTION).

BPMWE creates a local alkaline anode pH where OER catalysts have high selectivity and mitigate COR, combined with CEL limiting Cl- crossover due to cation transport selectivity (doc_0060_chunk_005581, RESULTS AND DISCUSSION).

Anodes comprising chromium-based alloys exhibit limited consumption during iron extraction and oxygen evolution by MOE due to the formation of an electronically conductive solid solution of chromium(III) and aluminium oxides in the corundum structure.

Oxygen evolution occurs at the chromium-based alloy anode during molten oxide electrolysis, confirmed by gas evolution at the electrolyte surface and oxygen content in the outlet gas comparable to noble-metal anodes.

The oxygen evolution reaction (OER) on LSCO perovskites involves a lattice oxygen-mediated mechanism (LOM) where lattice oxygen species and oxygen vacancies participate, differing from the traditional adsorbate evolution mechanism (AEM) that considers only transition metal redox activity.

Density functional theory modelling shows that increasing Sr substitution in La1-xSrxCoO3-δ reduces oxygen vacancy formation energy and bulk stability, leading to a transition from AEM to LOM mechanism for OER between x=0.25 and 0.5.

The LOM involves formation of superoxide-like –OO adsorbates and surface oxygen vacancies, with lower oxidation state of surface Co compared to AEM, resulting in higher stability and lower overpotential for OER on Sr-rich compositions.

Nickel hydroxide (Ni(OH)2) is used as a redox mediator to split conventional alkaline water electrolysis into two steps: hydrogen production and oxygen production, involving reversible cycling between Ni(OH)2 and NiOOH.
The alkaline water electrolytic cell is constructed with a Pt-coated Ti-mesh electrode for HER, a RuO2/IrO2-coated Ti-mesh electrode for OER, and a commercial Ni(OH)2 electrode located between them.
Step 2 (oxygen production): cathodic reduction of NiOOH to Ni(OH)2 and anodic oxidation of OH- to oxygen at the OER electrode.

The NPMC catalyst exhibits bifunctional electrocatalytic activity for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER), with ORR proceeding via a nearly four-electron pathway and OER showing lower onset potentials and higher currents than Pt/C and RuO2 nanoparticles; density functional theory calculations reveal that N,P co-doping and graphene edge effects are essential for this bifunctional activity (chunk_id=doc_0318_chunk_029726, section_title='').

First-principles calculations show that N,P co-doped graphene structures have minimum overpotentials of 0.44 V for ORR and 0.39 V for OER, lower than the best metal catalysts, indicating synergistic effects of N and P co-doping improve bifunctional catalytic activities; ORR and OER active sites are located near graphene edges but differ in location (chunk_id=doc_0318_chunk_029770, section_title='').

Some deterioration in rechargeable Zn–air battery performance occurs due to different catalytic activities of NPMC toward ORR and OER, causing irreversibility; however, performance can be improved by optimizing pore structure, doping sites, electrode chemistry, and cell configuration, such as using a three-electrode setup to separate ORR and OER electrodes (chunk_id=doc_0318_chunk_029765, section_title='').

The formation of the heterostructure of LSC and MoSe2 induces a local phase transition in MoSe2 from 2H to 1T phase, accompanied by electron transfer from Co to Mo. This electron transfer alters the Mo 4d-orbital configuration, inducing the phase transition and improving intrinsic conductivity of MoSe2, enhancing catalytic activity. The Co in LSC becomes more electrophilic, increasing Co–O and Co–OH amounts, improving overall water electrolysis performance. (chunk_id: doc_0349_chunk_032209, section_title: Results)

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations show charge transfer from LSC to MoSe2 (0.772 e), shifting the d-band center of Co upward by about 0.1 eV, enhancing electrophilicity and OH− affinity, improving OER catalytic performance. The d-band center of Mo shifts downward by ~0.9 eV, promoting 2H to 1T phase transition by destabilizing 2H-MoSe2. The energy gap closes in the heterostructure, enhancing intrinsic conductivity and HER catalytic performance. (chunk_id: doc_0349_chunk_032210, section_title: Results)
HER and OER profiles were investigated in N2-saturated 1 M KOH aqueous solution at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 by drop-coating catalyst ink onto glassy carbon disk electrode (area 0.13 cm2).

The in situ formed NiFe oxide and NiFe oxy(hydroxide) layers form a metal nitride-metal oxide/oxy(hydroxide) core-shell structure facilitating electron transfer from the NiFeN core to oxidized species, improving OER activity and chloride corrosion resistance. (doc_0368_chunk_033775, Results)

The oxygen evolution reaction (OER) occurs on the anode, competing with the chlorine evolution reaction (CER) due to chloride anions in seawater. High OER activity at overpotentials below 490 mV is required to avoid hypochlorite formation. (doc_0368_chunk_033746, )

Electrochemical oxidation of glucose produces glucaric acid and hydrogen gas simultaneously, with hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) at cathode and glucose oxidation at anode; glucose oxidation has lower standard redox potential (0.05 V) than water electrolysis (1.23 V), leading to energy savings; NiFeOx catalyst shows higher activity and selectivity for anodic glucose oxidation, while NiFeN catalyst shows higher activity for cathodic HER; glucose oxidation proceeds via oxidation of aldehyde and primary alcohol groups to carboxylic acids without C-C bond cleavage; in situ ATR-FTIR, NMR, and LC-MS analyses confirm intermediates and products including gluconic acid, glucaric acid, and guluronic

The electrocatalytic sulfion oxidation reaction (SOR) is used as a chlorine-free anodic reaction, thermodynamically favorable and with lower energy requirements than oxygen evolution reaction (OER).

The descriptor ARSC unifies activity and selectivity prediction for multiple electrocatalytic reactions including oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), oxygen evolution reaction (OER), carbon dioxide reduction reaction (CRR), and nitrogen reduction reaction (NRR). The descriptor captures atomic property, reactant, synergistic, and coordination effects on the d-band shape of dual-atom sites.

Operando EXAFS can identify fine structure of reconstruction-derived components by detecting shifts in bond lengths from oxidation behavior of metal species, effective in characterizing amorphous hydrous iridium oxide for OER.

The local bonding environments of CoOOH are similar to those of a CoPi catalyst consisting of similar CoO6 slabs, which meets the geometric requirement for the oxygen coupling mechanism (OPM). The (10–14) surface of CoOOH was used as a model surface in DFT calculations, considered the most stable surface. Surface reconstruction from CoOOH to CoO2 happens under OER conditions, but the local structure of reconstructed CoO2 is still lacking. The water structure changes with pH, affecting the super-Nernstian shift of the Co redox process and onset potential for OER. CoOOH is active across the whole pH range with pH-dependent redox features. Operando hard X-ray absorption spectroscopy shows a pH-induced change in Co oxidation onset but no change in coverage of redox-active Co species before OER. The pH-dependent catalytic performance is connected to interfacial Co oxidative transformations under electrocatalytic conditions. The kinetic isotope effect (KIE) measurements show a secondary KIE (~1) in both acidic and alkaline electrolytes, indicating proton transfer is not involved in the rate-determining step. The Co redox features shift super-Nernstianly with pH. Cyclic voltammograms show different Co redox peaks at pH ~1, 7, 10, and 13. Operando hXAS shows Co oxidation onset at ~1.20 V vs RHE in alkaline and ~1.40 V vs RHE in acidic electrolyte. Surface-sensitive soft XAS shows the surface is dominated by low-spin Co(III) species before and after OER, which reversibly oxidize to CoN species under OER conditions. The redox charge coverage is similar across pH, but the Tafel slopes differ (44.3 mV/dec in alkaline, 80.6 mV/dec in acidic), indicating faster kinetics in alkaline. The difference in activity is attributed to intermediate interactions rather than surface coverage.

Currently the two proven low-temperature (<100 °C) water electrolyser technologies dominating the commercial market are alkaline water electrolysis (AWE) and proton exchange membrane water electrolysis (PEMWE). Other emerging technologies include low temperature anion exchange membrane water electrolysis (AEMWE), as well as high-temperature electrolysis such as proton conducting ceramic electrolysis (~150–400 °C) and solid oxide electrolysis (~500–800 °C). The hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) at the cathode is a two electron-proton reaction: 2H+ + 2e- → H2 under acidic conditions, and 2H2O + 2e- → H2 + 2OH- under alkaline conditions. The oxygen evolution reaction (OER) at the anode is a multi-electron process: 2H2O → 4H+ + O2 + 4e- in acid, and 4OH- → 2H2O + O2 + 4e- in alkaline. Chloride oxidation reactions compete with OER in saline water, including chlorine evolution reaction (ClER) 2Cl- → Cl2 + 2e- at low pH, and hypochlorite formation Cl- + 2OH- → ClO- + H2O + 2e- at high pH.
